It is a great course. It not only introduces conventional textbook knowledge but also encourages and inspires us to explore new technologies and methods for homework and projects. The professor and TA respond quickly and are easy to communicate with.

The course is very well-structured, with comprehensive materials, including lectures, reading articles, videos, and discussions, all readily accessible. I appreciated how the professor paced the course, breaking down lectures into well-defined subtopics and assigning relevant reading materials, which made it easy to keep up.

Wonderful course that helped me to incorporate a lot of what I had learned both in industry and in my other coursework while collaborating with colleagues in other disciplines. This course does have a lot of assignments but due to the online nature of the course students are able to work ahead and keep things well paced.

Professor Ramani's dedication to the course is evident through his thorough feedback and helpful direction during discussions, which supplement the project work well. His passion for product design shines through, making real-world examples relatable and engaging

Pros: Insightful case studies, diverse project topics, exposure to real-world product challenges, opportunity to explore innovative solutions. Cons: Intense workload at times, heavy reading load for certain modules. Suitable for those interested in holistic design approaches

Takeaways: Great mix of theory and practical experience, with challenging projects that push you to think differently. Covers design principles, user needs, and market perspectives. The hands-on approach keeps things engaging, while teamwork helps build real-world skills. Highly recommended.

Great for mechanical engineers to grasp design thinking and decision-making. It's not about CAD but understanding principles behind good design. The course's real-world projects tie theory with practice, preparing you to innovate effectively. Professor Ramani's guidance broadens technical skills.

This class is about innovation and how you use your existing technical knowledge and experience to innovate and make something extraordinary. This class opens you to techniques which allow you to communicate your ideas better, evaluate your ideas, create proof of concepts, and make working models

This has been probably the best ME class I have taken till now! I really appreciate the amount of effort the professor and his coaching team have put in to design the course. This is
one of those courses which actually stimulates thinking. Many of the things that I have learned in this class will actually help me in my career.

Complements technical knowledge by teaching design thinking and multiple perspectives - end-user and market views. It equips students thrive in the business world, enhancing communication with stakeholders. The course offers a balance of theory and hands-on experience, including prototyping, teamwork, presentations, and time management.

Prof Ramani focuses in helping students get to the core concepts of design through lectures as well as through oractical experience of group projects. The course is a great balance of theory and practice. His advices and feedbacks are not narrow and just specific to question, but also tailored according to the needs of students.

As TA for the course, I noticed that students really appreciated how organized the course was. Dr. Ramani provided clear instructions and valuable feedback, making it easier for students to follow along and apply concepts to their projects.

I really enjoyed the lectures and found them engaging. Professor Ramani does a fantastic job of providing insightful reading materials before each module, which helps us grasp the course content better.

The course is very well-structured, and my group dynamics have worked perfectly, with a good group size that fosters collaboration. The material is organized and presented nicely on Brightspace, showing the effort that went into its design.

The course material is laid out really well, and I've been impressed by how responsive Professor Ramani and the teaching assistants are when we have questions. They're always available and flexible with design meetings, which makes a big difference.

Professor  Ramani,  you  are  the  man.  This  class  has  been  awesome  and  eye  opening  for  an  engineer  not  from  the  design  space. Chopping  the  lectures  into  the  smaller  modules  very  much  helps  with  absorbing  the  knowledge.  The  semester-long  project  reinforces  the  class  topics  as  the  course  progresses.

Professor made sure the course was well structured, so that students from all backgrounds could understand the course concepts. The examples and case studies used were very relevant,
which elevated the learning experience. I enjoyed the meeting times with the teacher and TA. Also, having the slack channels were helpful with the TA in each channel

Great course that is well structured to help us to develop a big picture in life. Prof: Ramani discusses, guides and refines our thoughts. Course contents have helped me to approach any problem creatively. Unique gem of a course being lead by a very dedicated Professor who wants to see all of his students become future innovators and entrepreneurs.

The class structure is largely based on the flipped classroom, meaning you teach yourself and he determines whether you've learned well enough. He did not ask questions well, and didn't give you a chance to clarify if he felt you didn't answer the question sufficiently, he would just give you no credit and move on.
His classes are engaging but heavy. If you welcome the challenge, you'll learn a lot.
I wish the lectures were more engaging.
His teaching style is not the best.The lectures can be difficult to follow.

This is actually for ME 351, as there was no option for this course. It's linear algebra. I have to say this is the only class I have EVER had trouble to the point to where I felt hopeless in learning the material. Exams were never remotely similar to any sort of material shown in class. Class scored below 60% on both midterm exams.
Extremely unprepared every class. Students who try to go to his lectures to learn will be out of luck.

Sometimes goes too fast during lecture, but overall he's not a bad teacher considering how difficult the class is. He's also a very nice person.

(Talks kind of like Dr. Doofenshmirtz)
Don't be fooled by how sweet and kind he comes off. Overall, will not be a good experience because he just doesn't teach well. You are in for a tough semester.
Tests are extremely difficult. Lectures are also unbearably boring and do not help at all in understanding material.

I had asked around and the averages for our section was ~20% lower than all other sections. This points to an underlying issue with the professor and it shows in how disengaged he is with students in his lectures. Very poor instruction overall and the worst teacher I have come across
Best advice I can give incoming students: Take with mummert if you can.

Absolute waste of my semester going to his lectures. All he does is write in silence and keep his head glued to his notes. Nothing else! No point in trying to go to his class, super unhelpful professor. Very bad look on Purdue hiring this guy... DO NOT TAKE AT ALL COSTS!!!

Really disappointed that this is the standard for Purdue Math Professors. I've had bad math teachers in high school, but I never realized that university could have even worse. I very much liked my other professors for chemistry and ENGR. Hopefully Purdue takes this into account.

I really do not think this professor is a bad guy at all. Genuinely seems very nice. Unfortunately he does not prepare any of us well for the exams. His communication skills are very hard to follow, he clearly does not put any planning into his lectures, and it just looks like he's in another world.
Big GPA Ruiner. Had to work really hard to just get a minimum mark for passing. Never wanna see him again.
Teaching style is very bad and clearly disregards the fact that people are suffering because of his poor lectures.
Should be fired. Actually makes me mad how bad of a teacher he was.
I don't know a single one of my friends who actually found his class useful. Now I will have to retake the class. I'm going to self study this time around because no way will I be going back.
So happy that I passed. My gpa standards lowered because of him.
I already retook the class so I ended up doing well this time around, but the professor was way worse than my previous one last semester.
Ended up learning more from the mylabmath examples than this guy. I suggest doing that instead.
The transition from Calc 1 and 2 was already hard enough. This guy made it impossible to learn anything.
Lectures = him quietly copying notes super fast while mumbling. Zero eye contact, zero interaction. Questions? Good luck. Self-teach from textbook if you take him.
Coming from other profs, he was a shock. Just scripts notes on iPad without looking up or elaborating.

This guy is a walking disaster for anyone trying to pass Calc 3. Head buried in his notes the entire time, writing furiously without a single explanation beyond a barely audible mutter. Questions? Forget it, he won't even glance up. Lectures are just him copying stuff super fast with zero context.
I genuinely learned more Calc 3 from YouTube comments than from these lectures.
Klehfoth gave cool demos. Class material is easy and theres so much extra credit. The content of PHYS172 is pretty boring tho, not his fault.

I enjoyed Professor Klehfoth's lectures. However, this was because I watched Rebello's pre-lectures and didn't try to take notes in lecture. I thought Klehfoth's demos were entertaining and due to the multitude of ways to earn points in 172 I did well in the class without acing the exams.
Honestly I never paid attention in lecture but bro is a chill dude
Reads off slides and is very monotone. People just sit and talk the whole lecture.

He's not a BAD lecturer, he does actually explain the concepts reasonably well, just at a mundane pace and tone (Go with rebello's ones nonetheless). He does do demos quite often w volunteers and he's actually pretty funny. He's no chen but these ratings are too harsh. It can be so much worse
Classic Klehfoth!

Lectures were pretty boring, and the professor didn't seem interested at all, though he did do some cool demos on occasion. You're probably better off just watching the videos that Sanjay Rebello makes. The course load is pretty heavy and the physics department kind of sucks in general, so taking physics elsewhere wouldn't be a bad idea.

Im pretty sure Klehfoth is relatively new to the whole teaching thing, because the guy does not know how to engage a class at all. He just reads directly off the slides. Lectures were completely pointless, better off watching Sanjay's videos. Occasionally he'll do a fun demonstration in class, so thats a plus

Many reviews call his lectures "monotone" and "boring".But  you've got to understand that: PHYSICS IS DRY (especially here at Purdue). After attending other classes, they are just as "boring". Professor Klehfoth does his best with the subject and successfully teaches the required material.

Mark Klehfoth has a basic, monotone lecture style. I'd liken his lectures to the scene in Monsters University where Mike and Sully attend the scream can design class after getting kicked out of the scare program.

Klehfoth is pretty monotone and can put you to sleep easily. In order to learn spend less time taking notes and really just absorbing what you see and try to make sense of it yourself. Do the clicker and challenge problems in lecture. Exams are easy if you do tons of previous exams as they stay the exact same year to year. AI did almost all my HW

Lectures were pretty monotone and a lot of the explanations didn't feel super clear, so I basically had to teach myself most of the class. He definitely seems really nice, but the stuff we covered in lecture didn't always feel connected to the exams, which made things a lot harder.

i have no personal grudge against this professor because I literally never showed up to any class (clicker can be done at home), but I just wanna say this class is hell on earth. I hate physics. Why do i have to pay for homework. i thought the tests were easy but like i somehow find a way to get 40 percent every single time. hella extra credit tho.

This guy is extremely monotone, and literally just yaps about the derivations on the slides without doing much explaination of conceptual stuff. Luckly, the class is very generous with extra credit and the exams are very similar to the practice exams posted on brightspace.

Professor Klehfoth is definitely knowledgeable about his topic. He demonstrates principles well during class with experiments, but is very monotone. Overall, the class was moderately easy, except for the final exam, which was extremely difficult. Around half of a midterm's worth of extra credit is handed out, and course is generous with leeway.

Easily the worst class/ teaching experience in my life. Lectures are dull, doesn't like answering questions, and speeds through the material (often not covering important topics that are needed to finish the homework which is also incredibly hard). Theres probably about 100-150 extra credit points available throughout the semester so you don't fail

Reads off the slides. Incredibly monotone. I rarely paid attention in class and it did not affect my grades in the slightest. On the bright side, his slides are useful for the problem quizzes every now and then. Strongly recommend another professor in class, though.
Just reads off the slides, doesn't even seem like he wants to teach whatsoever.

Lectures are focused on the simpler concepts instead of diving deeper into the challenging topics we are tested on. Demonstrations are interesting, and Klehfoth makes occasional jokes, but is super monotone, and lectures are bland, and not needed to succeed in the class.

Prof Klehfoth clearly knows his stuff and cares about it. He can have trouble sometimes answering simpler questions. I have heard students complain that he can be boring, but I can appreciate that he is straight to the point and does not waste time. Textbook only required for hw, which is much harder than the exams. I didn't watch pre/post lec vids
Very bland lectures, interesting demonstrations, but spent too much time on the more basic aspects than the harder material.
He only reads off the power points so I usually just play rainbet in class but, otherwise he's low-key terrible and hella unsendy.

Incredibly unengaging. He seems like a cool enough person, however, the class is completely unstructured and is taught in a way where the concepts don't seem to relate. When he "teaches," which is just reading off of the board, it is hard to follow because he rarely uses examples in class that related to the assigned homework.

Klehfoth's lectures are extremely unengaging. Most people study or do the homework while he "teaches" (reads off the slides). He puts in close to no effort into this class. I love physics, but honestly this class ruined it for me. Overall feels like a fake class.

PHYS172 is a difficult course in general due to the workload. There is so much to do every week- long labs and recitation worksheets, 35-45 HW questions per week, 1 quiz, etc. To top it off, Klehfoth's lectures are useless. I had to self-teach so much! The midterm breakdown and question types are released a week prior, so that is a plus. Good luck!
Prof. Shrivastav is a great communicator and focuses very much on the principles behind every system design.
Great professor. Very clear grading criteria and a very well organized course.
Great class and great professor! Focuses on the fundamentals and no need for prior networking class.
Great class to learn about the fundamentals of computer networking. Lectures are great and exams are fair. Ample time is given for all the programming labs.

This was easily the most structured and well organized course that I have taken at Purdue. Lectures are amazing. The prof. truly cares about teaching and teaches with great enthusiasm. Grading is very fair and transparent.
Lectures are on point. Exams test your understanding of the subject. Attends to all questions that students have with precision.

A very well organized class. I opted for research project even as an undergrad and had a great experience working with a grad student the entire semester. Grading is very fair, though professor does not provide any practice tests for exams.
Great lectures and the workload is very manageable. I had taken 463 before, so this class seemed like a natural extension. You will learn a lot about state of the art practices.
Great class and great professor. Teaches lot of relevant stuff if you want to work for large companies. Class load is relatively low.

This was a very well organized course and the professor taught with great enthusiasm. I learned a lot about datacenter and cloud network which is very relevant for my future career. Grading and exams were very reasonable.

I liked this course as I think it teaches a lot of relevant stuff. Networking is a key component of any system today, and the professor does a great job of teaching the fundamentals from first principles. Although, I was not a big fan of grading. All labs are auto-graded and so even if you make a small mistake the auto-grader might give you a 0.
Great class and a great professor! I perhaps learned more in this class than any other class at Purdue. Labs and exams are not easy, but professor applied a very generous curve at the end.
It was a fun class. Most of the grade was based on a semester long project, which I really enjoyed. I learned a lot about networking in the real world from this class.

Not a hard class even though it is a 6000-level class. The material is easy to understand if you already have taken the undergrad networking class. The exams were very fair and the semester-long project was also very doable. I found the material very relevant to industry jobs in networking.

A relatively easy class in terms of workload. Lots of 1-1 interactions with the Prof. Lectures were great. 60% of the grade was based on a semester long project. I chose the research track, and it was quite fun. Midterm and final exams could be a bit tricky if you do not study the material properly, as there are no practice exams or assignments.

Even though this was a grad class, I found it relatively easy, partly because Professor Shrivastav did a great job with the lectures. Most of the grade is based on a semester long project. I chose implementation track, and it was very doable. Overall a great class!
I took 463 in Fall and 695 in Spring. This class was a perfect continuation of 463. I learnt a lot of advanced stuff about networking. Prof. was amazing just like in 463.

It was a really fun class and you learn lots of useful content. Attendance is not mandatory but I will highly recommend attending the lectures regularly, otherwise it is easy to loose track of the course material. Professor really cares about learning and spends a lot of time answering questions in class.

Great lectures but very exam heavy (75% of the grade is based on 3 exams). So if you are not good at timed-exams you will be in trouble. But otherwise, it was definitely one of the best classes I took at Purdue.

Prof. Shrivastav is the best professor I have had at Purdue. He teaches with so much enthusiasm and energy that I really looked forward to his lectures. The course material was challenging but doable. The labs were fairly easy but exams can be tough. Grading was fair.

Honestly, this is the best class I have taken in ECE. The lectures are absolutely amazing and the material is both engaging and extremely useful. I highly recommend this class to everyone in ECE. You will have to put in the work, but honestly it will be totally worth it in the end. The cut-offs for letter grades is also very generous.

This is a hard class, as expected for an upper-level ECE elective. But professor does a great job of explaining the material. You learn a lot of useful stuff too. Be prepared to put in the time, but overall I found this class extremely rewarding.

One of the most content heavy courses I have taken. The professor makes a point to ensure that your questions are answered and will even spend up to 10 mins mid-lecture answering questions and clearing any concerns on content. Overall a well read and caring guy, but the exam heavy grading + immense content to interpret made the course hard.

Good professor for the content. Kinda expects students to have a near photographic memory and grading on tests wasn't flexible bc he expected that. It's a TON of content too - informational but so much packed in one sem. So overall prof/class decent, but I think the grade makeup was ridiculous - 75% from 3 exams, 25% from 2 (or optionally 4) labs

Great professor for a relatively tough class. Very useful information even outside of computer networking with things like priority queues and distributed programming. As long as you go to lectures the class is doable. A few of the labs were tough but the grade cutoffs are pretty fair.
Makes content overly complicated, the homework isn't always relevant, or feels like busywork. If you have one on one questions he is helpful, but not always so if ask during lecture.
The homework and quizzes are unrelated to what you're working on in class, prof gets off topic everyday and is unwilling to meet with students when needed.

The class was challenging but I felt like he was very understanding and worked with the class. He adjusted his grading when we were all doing bad. There are some topics that are hard but I think his lectures plus the textbook explained them pretty well. I really enjoyed this class and Dr. Francis is an interesting guy. Talk to him outside of class!

Dr. Francis made this class way harder than it had to be. I feel like I know absolutely nothing about phonetics. I do not feel prepared for grad school. He even admits Praat is not going to be significant for us in our careers, but the whole class revolves around Praat. It's awful. Take with someone else if you can.

The professor himself was kind and wanted the students to do well. Be sure to ask questions if you are struggling. You HAVE to know how to use Praat so pay attention. The lectures were disorganized, and instructions were vague. I really feel like I didn't learn anything about actual Phonetics.

This was by far my least favorite class I've taken at Purdue for (Phonetics) Hoe to use the program Praat was unclear and confusing. I did not like this class at all and found the lectures unorganized and instructions on how to use praat was difficult and unclear even after watching the lecture videos serveral times. Be careful in this class

Dr. Francis is such a sweet man, though his class was really challenging to navigate. Course is primarily based on Praat labs. We were never really taught how to use the software and the instructions for the labs were very vague. Seek help early if you are struggling! He will most definitely get you to where you want to be.

Dr. Francis was great. This class was not much work and both him and our TA were very helpful with answering student questions. As long as you either actually attend class or watch the lecture recordings you should be fine. Without doing that you won't know how to use Praat which is crucial.

I have no idea why his rating is so low. I am in this class now and should be getting an A or A+. There is only one assignment per week and he usually dedicates almost a whole class a week to answering questions/going over it. He is very nice and happy to answer student's questions. There is a class participation component but also extra credit.

I assume that people who thought the assignments were difficult are those that did not attend lectures. Go to class and you'll do great. He literally walks you through most of the assignments. They were straightforward and fair based on the course content. Just dedicate some time upfront to learning to use Praat and you'll sail through the class.

If sometime in the future there is another professor teaching phonetics, take it with them! do not take this class unless you have to. it is such a headache, and you will learn absolutely nothing about phonetics. i am scared of going into grad school with people from other undergrad programs because i will be so behind in my knowledge of phonetics

This Professor does not care about teaching at all. I know many people in the class who tried emailing him about unfair grading, and he responded only after grades were finalized. This class should be called intro to Praat, because you will learn nothing about phonetics except how to use that software.

Really frustrating class. Dr. Francis is smart and friendly but is not a great teacher. Homework has unclear instructions, takes forever, and is graded seemingly randomly. Class is far more focused on learning how to use the system Praat than on learning any real phonetics information. Emailing doesn't get you much either, and TA was pretty useless

Class and professor were very frustrating. Got very little direction for lab assignments & final project. So many lab assignments that were only out of 10 points. I would spend hours on 1 assignment, miss 3 points & get a 70%. Never heard from the TA. She was only in class once at the beginning of the semester. This class was very disorganized.

I have attended most if not all of the classes that we have had and I am still not sure what to do in this class to succeed. I have worked very hard on all my labs and homework assignments and they are only worth 10 points each. I could work for hours on one assignment (checking each problem with the professor) and would still manage to get a C.
I am not sure what my grade is in this course, despite it being the past the last week of class.
This is the worst professor I have ever had. It is the week before finals and I still have no idea what my grade is in class.
He is a really good guy and is very knowledgable but gets distracted and goes into long tangents. Attendance isn't mandatory and he uploads videos of the lectures which is nice.
The worst professor I have ever had.

Dr. Francis genuinely cares for his students! Go in with an open mind and show up. There were no exams, just hw, labs, and the final project that he helps guide you through. The class can be frustrating at times, but it is possible to get a good grade in the course, so don't be scared and ask for help, he will be more than happy to do so.

This prof. is a super nice guy, and is very knowledgeable. He simply cannot teach. This class consisted of us using Praat, which basically is a bunch of spectrograms and wavelengths of sounds you input. We had no hw or tests which was nice, but all we did was time-cosuming labs. I learned nothing in this course besides how to use praat.

Dr. Francis is a really nice guy; he loves to talk about grad school and he's a good person to know because he's on the admissions committee! That being said, he should not teach undergrad courses. He goes on tangents about thing that are TOTALLY irrelevant and the exams are TOUGH. Additionally, he frequently forget to post content so beware

teaches the class at a graduate level- in lab with TA is the only time you learn anything helpful. Will skip over important materail and talk about things that you won't be tested on instead. Spent hours studying and still didn't do well. Very project heavy and time consuming. Bless the TA otherwise no one i know wouldv'e passed the class.

The tests were brutal. Many students I knew in the course were retaking the course after not passing and some are retaking it after not passing. Be prepared to work for this grade. He does answer your questions during office hours and can be really helpful, he is a nice guy, just an extremely hard class.

He is quite possibly the worst professor I've ever had. He knows what he's talking about but he talks over your head and could care less what you do and don't understand. His tests are all short answer and extremely subjective. His lectures are not mandatory but his labs are because there are weekly quizzes.

Long lectures, often taught by his TA. Homework (class study) feels unnecessary, but if you attend the last class of the week, his TA basically gives you all the answers. Use those to help you prepare for his exams, and take advantage of the cheat sheets he allows.

After about the 2nd week his TA's took over class.. All the slides are posted. If you go to the review classes and make a solid formula sheet the exams are a breeze, and they do the in-class assignments worth half your grade in class. Overall not the worst class but I would prefer if Prof. Karanki taught instead of his TAs.

Did class study every week during class so show up and he does iclicker. They work through the class study during class and give you the answers. Karanki made going to a 7:30 class more than tolerable, he would bring us breakfast on exam review days and hand out gift cards if we participated in class. The exams are all online and multiple choice.

I'm pretty sure Prof. Karanki is a really nice person. The thing this semester though is that after the first week he stopped teaching and let his TA's take over, which is one thing if done well but since the class administration is bad then I don't think I can recommend Karanki until he himself shows back up to class to teach.

The professor teaches the course well and is very engaging with students. He dedicates one lecture before any take home exam to give us a simulated practice exam that we work together on as a class. Be prepared for the 10+ page group essay at the end of the semester.

His exams feel subjective, as it will include questions like "which word best describes this scenario". Karanki's classes are lecture heavy, but gives out lots of small extra credit points. He gives everyone who gets 100% on each exam a gift card. Make sure you take lots of notes, and make formula sheets for the exams.

He was a very fun and inspiring person to learn from. If he taught any other class I would have loved it. Unfortunately the class he teaches is so boring, and irrelevant for the major that requires it, it makes it impossible to enjoy this amazing proffessor

He is clearly very passionate about aviation and truly loves his job, which makes this lecture-heavy class quite enjoyable. However, some test questions were worded really strangely and didn't really make any sense. For example, pick the word that best fits, but then you would have two words that mean the same thing.

Dr. Karanki is a really nice professor.  It is clear that he cares about all of his students and he somehow managed to remember 70+ names within a few weeks.  His lectures have a lot of good information, and he often invites valuable industry connections to present in class.

Professor Karanki is one of the nicest professors in SATT. He actively makes an effort to build a relationship with each of his students, even though his class is very lecture-heavy. He offers lots of extra credit AND he brings candy to his review sessions. If you pay attention during lectures, you will succeed.

Fecri was a really nice guy I think his class just was really boring. He gives out a lot of extra credit opportunities and does his best to learn everyone's names. He also does a good job answering questions. Just really lecture heavy. Easy tests and quizzes.
Long lectures covering content not always relevant. Would ask if students have questions, but would not answer those questions clearly, often making the content even more confusing than necessary.

I took 410 and 410 with him. Uses his own book which is exactly what he teach in the class, and both are not very helpful. Doesn't really answers the questions students asks (doesn't like being questioned repeatedly). Few questions on homework but they take forever. Grading is pretty easy though. He is basically the typical old physics professor.

(PHYS410 H Mech I is now defunct) For intermediate mechanics, Nolte teaches from his own textbook, which is adequate but not ideal (the good part is beyond the scope of a non-honors class). When answering questions, he tends to talk in circles until the questioner gives up. Class is difficult and time consuming, but the grading is generous.
Not a big fan of his teaching style.
Hes a great professor but my god the homework and exams are long. Dont expect any plans over the weekend if you have them. Most do fine just the typical Purdue physics course

He is great at teaching the material and is very clear about the things you should expect on the exams and how to prepare for them. The class isn't easy but its very fair. If you do the work, go to lecture, and study, you'll do fine.

He knows the material however seems to forget that his students are taking this course for the first time. Class average was consistently a D the entire semester. Very test heavy and likes to try to trip up students with his questions. Be ready to have to teach yourself and pay attention in the lectures. Avoid at all costs.
He gives good explanations of the material, and he is interesting and lively when he lectures.  A very good professor overall.
Nolte takes the act of teaching the material more seriously than any other professer I've had here.  If you have him, you'll get to enjoy the process of learning again.

Dr. Seagraves was an amazing professor, and clearly super passionate about what he does. The class itself is not hard at all, as long as you read the readings before classes and show up to class. He's a fair grader and generally pretty lenient.

My original professor was replaced with him and tbh, was not a great fan since he focused on economic readings but he is a nice professor. He is very engaging and passionate on what he teaches. He is also caring and not a tough grading. Just do the readings cause there is pop writing quizzes sometimes but they are not bad.

Professor Seagraves was one of my favorite teachers despite the fact I didn't love public speaking. He truely cares about the students in his classes and grades based on how you've improved over the semester rather than some random standard.

Really engaging professor, he draws your attention really well and actually cares about each and every student. Phones are not allowed in class which helps with engagement.  Uses all 50 minutes to a maximize, amazing professor

He certainly cares a lot about the course he teaches and goes out of his way to engage with the class and know his students. The biggest complaint is we had projects for his class run into the middle of finals week, which felt like a bit much. Do the reading, and when giving speeches, body language is key.

Great professor for SCLA102. He's a pretty young professor so he's very cheerful and nice to talk to. He's super into the topics he teaches and so his lectures are enjoyable. Two impromptu speeches (which are pretty easy) and two big group projects (which are easy if you have a good group). If you go to class, and do the reading, it's an easy A.

Unlike most professors I had this semester, Dr. Seagraves isn't a boring lecturer who talks in a monotonous tone but rather is very engaging. He also goes out of his way to learn EVERY student's name. Seagraves is always willing to help you understand concepts. However, there is a lot of reading, and he may grade a bit harsh on some speeches.
Samperton is a great professor, gives easy to follow lectures, the content of MA261 is just difficult.
Great lectures and a great professor.
Professor Samperton is the goat.

An absolute GOAT, this man is my hero. I've have many classes with him now, and he always knows the subject matter extremely well, is kind and inviting, is always open for questions, is a good lectures and makes the content very easy to digest.

In short, he's great. Highly recommend. And no he didn't pay me for this, I just think he's awesome.

Prof. Samperton is very passionate in teaching. His diff topo mainly follows Lee's book, but more than replicating definitions& theorems from book, he always explains the motivation and intuition behind vividly, which makes the lectures easy to follow with sufficient depth. He also posts notes, quiz study guide, lecture videos on his website.
Takes himself too seriously. His ideas about differential topology and proofs did not seem original enough. The course structure also seemed too strict, and rushed.

I didn't go to class too much because I just watched Chenflix for notes but Samperton is a really chill professor. Feels super easy to approach if you have any questions about notes, homework, or test prep. Tests are pretty challenging but that's not really Samperton's fault. Overall great person.

I had a really great time taking AAE418 in Spring 2026. He also has very good TAs that help run the course. The projects definitely simulate real world problems and Prof. C also has a very interesting sense of humor. Be prepared to work independently and ask him a lot of questions.

The class is very interesting and Prof. Collicott allows you to figure things out before you are to report back to him about your findings. If you are prepared and do the necessary tasks to keep your project going, you are in a good place to get a good grade. Just be proactive and be responsible.
He clearly knows what he is doing. A learnt a lot being in his class.
Has no heart! I have no idea how he's a professor. Purdue do better!

Steven Collicott is the single most disrespectful person I have ever met. He berates students for not explaining themselves correctly. AAE 418 is a design class, but you have zero room to design anything. If you take ANY creative liberties, Steven Collicott will rudely shut you down because you are not creating his design.

This class would be one of the best at Purdue if it wasn't for such a disrespectful and rude professor. Berated and embarrassed me in front of my group because my idea was 'too complicated'. Told us to use his idea, which was overly complicated, so we used my design. At the end of the course, he said the mechanism I created worked flawlessly.

AAE418 was the single worst experience I had at Purdue. Dr. Collicott was disrespectful, passive-aggressive, and rude to everyone on my team. The projects are all very interesting and provide valuable experience, but the prof completely ruins the experience. I cannot in good conscience recommend this class so long as Dr. Collicott is "teaching" it.
Ruse and very passive-aggressive. He never really tells you what he wants to have done and rarely listens to the students.

Dr. Collicott is impatient and even rude. His general demeanor is one of arrogance and disdain. No amount of kindness can change his attitude. It is clear that he is installed at Purdue only to rattle on and on about his research. One might even be surprised if he didn't send himself trophies. You have been warned.

If you want a comprehensive understanding of fluid mechanics, do not take it with Steven Collicott. Spends at least 50 % on average of class time talking about his research. and does not seem to know what is on the homework. Encouraged my class to buy his book instead of the universal Anderson book, which would have been more helpful.
If you get a tiny bit wrong on your paper, he will cross out your ENTIRE WORK leaving no respect. Worst professor ever.
His teaching is bad. He talks to himself, and ends class. multiple choice exam was very bad. Avoid by all means.

Classes can be variable. Some are focused on topic, but others could consist of 35 minutes worth of his life story. Homework is due 3 times a week, and does take quite the effort to complete. The exams are incredibly brutal. MC on this level of engineering? Come on. Also, they are entirely theoretical. Know how to derive every single equation.

He knows a lot and by that I mean very high level stuff so he has very little interest in teaching a junior level class like 333. He talks about stuff without introducing it to the students beforehand expecting that we all know it like he does. TA Jaime is very helpful. If you visit him once a week you'll be fine. Tests are MCQ which is ridiculous.

Due to his oversight, the final exam differed in breadth and difficulty so much in comparison to previous exams that it resembled what a course 6-5 student might take and not a Purdue EE student. Additionally, his teaching style cannot be called a style. His field of expertise lies in research and working with graduate students, not undergraduates.

Had him as a recitation TA but just had to rate here because wow. Made someone cry on the first day of RECITATION (didn't know that was possible). Became the course coordinator for 2k1 halfway through the semester and the final exam genuinely had people praying to anyone that would listen after the second midterm already had an average of 50%.
The content of the tests borderlines esoteric compared to that from past 2k1 professors. Be aware, anyone who is taking it next semester.

The slides are detailed and helpful, but the lectures themselves aren't very effective. You'll likely need to spend a good amount of time reviewing the material and textbook on your own outside of class.

Probably the worst prof I have ever had. I swear he made the content harder to understand than it was. The slides are detailed and good for reference, but they are very busy and difficult to follow in a lecture. He is comically bad at answering students' questions. Seemed like a nice guy, just not a good teacher.

I wholeheartedly disagree with some of the reviews that say Prof. Kildeshev is mean - he has been one of the kindest professors I've ever had. He genuinely cares about his students' success and has a deep understanding of the content. His lectures can be a little boring, but he really is much better than some of the reviews make him out to be.

As a recitation instructor, he's often quite mean to students when they ask questions he deems "dumb," even when they are valid questions. He often goes on little ego trips. You will fall asleep during his lectures. His redeeming qualities are that he is very smart and knows what he's talking about, and his slides are very detailed and helpful.

One of the worst professors I've ever had. He picks on struggling students and is extremely tough to understand because of his disorganized slides. I'd be better off spending an hour studying and reading the textbook on my own instead of sitting in his lectures (I only attended for the recitation bonus).

During lecture he will get frustrated by students for taking notes on their laptop or tablets, talking, or asking questions he thinks are dumb. The class was challenging and his lectures were just ok.

I honestly felt like I learn more from books, videos, and just about any other resource other than him. He has a strong accent, speaks very slow so it's hard to really keep track of what was happening for me at least, and he'll expect you to find the material easy with the way he teaches (it's not). I won't be taking it with him next time.

This was probably one of my least favorite professors of all time. He gets frustrated when students don't find the material as simple as he does, and he will pick on students struggling in the class. This includes him talking about how poorly a specific, named student did on an exam in front of the entire class. Will not take a class of his again.

He only liked some people in the class. If he didn't like you, he pretended you didn't exist. He ignored the two emails I sent him. His lecture slides are helpful whether or not you attend class. Overall, he is probably a better bet than most 201 professors

Dr. Kildishev is one of the better ECE 201 professors at Purdue. His lectures can be a bit hit or miss, but if you read the textbook beforehand, you'll be fine. Some will say he's rude, but if you pay attention in class and do what you're supposed to do, you have nothing to worry about. He does pick on students that are struggling in the class.

I enjoyed the lectures and the class wasn't that hard, but it's objectively true that his lectures aren't the most relevant to exams and you'll have to study outside of class. It's unfortunate that his lectures have mandatory attendance.

This guy just talks about random stuff instead of teaching the material well. His style is just really bad. He claims that his lectures are a "reverse learning environment" but provides the most basic examples that don't reinforce learning at all. He only goes over topics at surface level too. you have to learn with the textbook and videos.
The lectures were okay and I was able to do well on exams with his lecture slides as study material.

The best thing for the students is that Prof. Klimeck is finally retiring. Can you imagine a class where students are required to be physically present and forbidden from using their phones, yet the instructor, the great Prof. Klimeck, joins via Zoom while on vacation and spends the time talking at length about his retirement plans?

ECE 694 marketed as a job-search course but only 1–2 of 16 sessions actually cover it. Rest is retired profs telling success stories. Mandatory attendance + QR surveys. I've had to reschedule real interviews for this. Resumes get distributed to the whole class (privacy concerns). Make-ups push nanoHub, a platform Klimeck directs.

Prof. Klimeck is a wealth of information and makes himself available to students in a way I have seen no other professor do. The course material was challenging, but Prof. Klimeck clearly wants his students to succeed. His attention to detail and responsiveness was an incredible breath of fresh air. Be prepared to work... a lot. But it's worth it.

606 with Klimeck was the most difficult class I ever took, but also the most rewarding. Lectures are recorded online, there are two recitations a week to ask questions (one with TA, one with prof), and there is a good textbook. There are two big projects (very hard), an exam (hard), and homeworks (hard but completion) that you're graded on.

Attendance is strictly mandatory. He will not count your attendance if you are on your phone. Expect to significantly improve your resume while ranking yourself against your classmates. However, he does prepare a list of good speakers, about 40% were really good, 15% really boring.

Great professor with a solid intensity to the course. He challenges you but also helps you succeed through extra credit and clear expectations. Really appreciate how invested he is in students learning.

Very challenging yet super interesting course as it gives good concrete exposure to the fascinating world of semiconductor devices. Different topics covered under the vast syllabus of this course find application in 2 well-defined projects. Recitation sessions are helpful and instructor is very responsive, supportive & caring for the students.

Prof. Klimeck strives for excellence, making this a demanding but enjoyable course. It is perfect for budding device engineers due to its heavy focus on the intricate details of physics. However, for those seeking a broad overview, the depth can lead to losing interest. I highly recommend having a solid exposure to quantum physics before enrolling

Prof. Klimeck is a great teacher/mentor. His structure for ECE 694 is quite thoughtful especially the module on Financial Literacy for grad students. I enjoy his presentations and the way he organizes the course. I just feel that most of the presentations on Nanohub are quite old and could make for renewed Presentations from later years.

I am not on for attending non-technical seminars, but this course has a great range of talks. I can count on one hand the amount of talks I disliked or didn't find helpful. The resume exercises were great. The only issue was that the professor could be really direct - which is not a bad thing- but I am not a fan of the way he conveys the message.
He does good job creating some interest in an otherwise uninteresting course

Prof Klimeck might seem strict but that is because he is serious about the course and wants the students to take something out of it. The lectures were interesting and mostly focused towards professional development. He did a very good job of analyzing the data from the resume voting activity.
Professor Gerhard's aura and the amazing lectures from other faculty truly made this course very interesting and definitely worth taking.
Prof. Klimeck clearly put a lot of effort into making it more than just your generic seminar course.

Prof. Klimeck talks a lot of content that is helpful to grad students, and his speaking is clear and not fast so that you can catch up easily as a non-native speaker. Prof. Klimeck also devotes a lot of time to preparing the courses even though he has taught in other semesters. I appreciate all the efforts

Took the graduate seminar with Prof. Klimeck. I thought I was going to hate the course (like I had every other seminar before this one) but it's clear Prof. Klimeck puts lots of effort into this class. I am surprised at how much I actually enjoyed the class.

he is a detailed person, I learned a lot from him and his course, but it was time-consuming 30+ hours a week of lectures, solving quizzes, 9 homework, recitations (very useful),  2 projects, and 4 peer reviews (Very active learning experience) but cons: 3.5 hours per review. Also, 4 additional reviews, believe me, my friend, you want this bonus.
Professor is experienced and looks to help out all the students grow fairly.
Enjoyed his talks. Inspirational and clearly outlines guidelines for a successful PhD and after should-do tips.

The course both expanded and deepened my technical/physical knowledge and skills.  Professor is eager to explain concepts, one can address him with a research issue and get a pointer in the right scientific direction. Some homework solutions are more connected with mandatory readings. Group projects were an enriching and insightful experience.
The professor is great. It's me who's dying. Not good for mental health. I feel like we don't deserve this course.

VERY challenging course requiring ~25 hours per week for lectures, readings, quizzes, and HW.  HW is challenging requiring a lot of time.  Put in the work and you will succeed.  Group projects are great for fully grasping the material, assuming you don't have dead weights in your group forcing you to do the work on your own. Lots of extra credit!

I had Prof Klimeck for 606 and it has been my favorite at Purdue thus far! I'm in the military so I have weird, long work hours but I found the lectures/readings very interesting (and relevant) and I used that knowledge on the group projects--which were HIGHLY valuable, and are why the class has been so meaningful to me, 10/10 would recommend!

Really good professor! Enjoyed learning the material. One suggestion would be to have individual office hours? Asking question in class can be overwhelming for me. Other than that, enjoyed the class and really appreciate the projects instead of exams. Learnt so much more that way instead of trying to memorize formulas and so.

Excellent professor.  He makes time to really make sure his students know what's going on in class, and is very fair with providing students a lot of ways to succeed.

The class is a lot of work, but that's because the subject matter is difficult- it is very well balanced by the professor's zoom sessions, and quick grading and feedback.

Professor Subbarayan is one of the best professors I have ever had. His passion for continuum mechanics and his clear, ground up explanations made his Fall 2024 class the only real source of joy during a difficult start to my PhD. He is an exceptional teacher and an easy going person. I highly recommend his course.

Professor Subbarayan teaches exceptionally well, and I am greatly indebted to him. He understands the material deeply, and I liked his mathematical style. I wished for more discussion of algorithms, but the projects and Abaqus work taught me how a finite element solver is built. The foundation helped me later in a fluid mechanics FE course.

Extremely through mathematical approach of the material. I took this class a few years ago and now have a career in this exact field. I find I'm better than many coworkers at my job because of how fundamental a level I learned this at. Very difficult exams though, designed so that no one can get 100% and then curved to the highest grade.

Exams test your ability to derive tensor identities and prove theorems, not solve real-world problems. Avoid this class if you're looking for practical application rather than theory for its own sake.

Took his class in Fall of 25. Definitely a graduate level continuum mechanics class, but I really enjoyed his lectures. He always took lots of time to explain why we should care about this material and did a good job making the course content relevant to all research interests.

Took during Spring 20. All three exams (best of two is usually chosen) were tricky, but not hard per se. Course introduces practical statistical techniques engineers employ to design for reliability.

Brush up your basics in probability theory - I hadn't, and found it a little hard. There were 2 projects as well (coding) one of which you could drop

Took the course in Fall 21. It introduces the concepts of Tensor calculus and index notations for the first few lectures, and later delves into kinematics and so on. 

Very applicable for those delving into applied research, and want to get comfortable reading similar research papers. Test is similar to homework -conceptual and tricky, but not hard

Choice of textbook is awful. Professor is very hands off and scarecly accessible outside of class, and offloads the MAJORITY of his workload to his single TA. Assigned a final project that was not covered in Lab, coded in MATLAB which the professor claims to not even be familiar with when asked.

Unusable content for real live scenarios. Boring style lecture videos.  Very hard and long exams, completely  different to homeworks and examples.  The time on exams in never enough.  Sadly I felt I wasted my money.
I do not recommend this course, and it was one of the worst classes for me at Purdue University.

Very detailed and organized. Always was willing to take time in class for "digressions" and answer questions. Has office hours upon request only and will answer questions if you message him (although the TA was very helpful as well). Exams and projects are fair but can take some thought.

The HW problems are way easier than the exam questions - so they don't really prepare you well for the exam. Professor does not hold office hours - only the TA does. The TA is good. There's an optional project which can replace a low score on an exam. HW, and 3 exams, and optional project. Overall, class is good.

The examples he teaches in the video lectures is simple but he asks way too tough question in the homework. And the homework requires Matlab/octave but the exam questions are totally different from those questions.

This online class is by far the most unorganized class I have ever taken. For homework assignments, the professor does not give solutions, so even if you do get a question wrong, you won't know how you got it wrong. 90% of questions in the lecture slides use Octave so you can't study for exams using them. Do not take this course with this professor

The professor was very thorough in all of his derivations and made sure that everyone was able to follow. He slowed down the pace of the course to accommodate for people seeing material for the first time. HW graded for completion so less stress, but you still need to think about the problems to make sure you're ready for the tests!

Id rather get coronavirus virus than continue dealing with this class. Im sure its usually fine to take but the way hes adjusted it due to the current virus situation makes it miserable and unreasonable.

Great teacher! Teaches everything from basic concepts, goes through the course very methodically.  Homeworks and tests were designed in a way to enhance the learning curve. The projects were very helpful, gave a very practical point of view to the subject.
Participation matters. Term projects are practical, interesting and valuable.

Assignments given every lecture. Gets extremely tedious near the end to just keep writing and writing whatever you can. The only grade that you need to try for is the midterm. Whatever you get on the midterm will decide your grade for the course since the other assignments are easy. Very helpful when asked about the project.

Professor Labi is an outstanding instructor. He's willing to stay after class to answer questions and is happy to meet outside of class as well. What really stands out is that he offers constructive feedback on assignments before they're due, giving you a real opportunity to improve your work rather than just receiving comments after submission.

I like Labi, so its a shame that this course is poorly taught. He is caring and funny, but was always unprepared for class. Lectures use old slides, are poorly explained, and dont cover necessary content.

He forgot about the midterm, so he just cancelled it. may not sound like a big deal, but its representative of how the whole course is taught.

Professor Labi is a good guy and I think he cares about his students, but his number one priority is clearly not this class. There is a semester long project, and you can expect to get very little guidance on what you need to do. Annoying but easy in-class quizzes make attendance mandatory. Still, homework is optional and the workload is light.

I love Dr Labi. I think he has such a great heart and genuinely cares about his students which is tough to find at Purdue these days. That being said, beware if he is gone and has a TA lecture, they are not great. There are in-class activities each lecture which are for a grade and you will need to attend. Just read the slides though, they're easy.
Very disorganized and rude in his emails. Says class is an "easy A". It is not. With that said, you will have no problem passing if you keep up with the work, but it will not be fun.

There is a lecture quiz for every lecture due at the end of class, but he gets very upset when students are working on the quiz while he's teaching or asking questions about it. Not sure what he expected, sometimes he'll help with the questions but most gets mad when you ask about them. It's still a relatively easy A/B, just annoying

Sam Labi is a nice guy, but that was the only positive thing about his class. He was constantly late to class or simply not present. It is clear that teaching the class was not a priority for him. Lectures were a waste of time, but required due to in class exercises. Group project was 60% of the grade and little feedback was given during semester.

It is hard to rate him as he wasn't there for half the class because he was in Hong Kong. It didn't feel like he cared about the class that much. Pop quizzes almost every class period so you have to attend. Lectures are very boring. Exams were open book open note. The group project wasn't too much work, and is graded very easily.
He uses innovative teaching techniques to explain difficult material very well. Shows genuine concern for students.
Great person!
He grades easy but make sure you make a genuine attempt to learn the stuff because he will know otherwise.
Extremly nice human being. A clean heart person, difficult to find such human in today materialistic world
No Comments
Dr. Labi is a good teacher and a really nice guy.

He is the best (G.O.A.T), period. His passion for fluid mechanics is infectious. I consider myself lucky to be able to attend his lectures. Although I wished he had followed Gary Leal's book instead of pantons.
He teaches very well. I love the fact that he teaches everything from first principles. He will take you through the derivations and help you understand well.

Definitely a solid instructor. No exams—just two take-home assignments that are very manageable if you follow the lectures. The assignments are based on what's taught in class, so nothing feels out of the blue. He's really good at organizing and explaining the material, and the resources he provides are super helpful and practical.

There are no exams—just two take-home assignments that are very manageable if you follow the lectures. The assignments are based on what's taught in class, so nothing feels out of the blue. He's really good at organizing and explaining the material, and the resources he provides are helpful and practical. Definitely a solid instructor.
Good grading system

This is a fair course with fair effort = 10 hours per week (lecture + HW). Matlab is used but not heavily. Lectures recorded from in-person class. Collaboration for HW is allowed (highly encourages a discord). Exams are solo "homeworks" but slightly more challenging. Relevant material. Good course (recommended) and Christov is a good instructor.

Prof Christov is great. Fluids is one of those topics in ME I'm usually worried about, and not super interested in. The way he teaches the class however is awesome, and I thoroughly enjoyed it. His enthusiasm for the subject is definitely infectious, and I think that's a hallmark of a great lecturer.
One of the best professors I have taken throughout all of my undergraduate and postgraduate co

Professor Christov excels in working through example problems similar to the HW and exams but teaches like the students already know the material. Lectures are super dense/fast-paced so if you haven't read ahead and understand what's going on it's easy to get lost. Knowledgeable professor, but you'll have to supplement.

ME-30900 is a difficult class and Professor Christov is an alright lecturer. He is helpful in office hours, but I had a difficult time learning the material and I didnt learn well going to his lecture.

Professor Christov is an excellent lecturer. He connects the book knowledge to actual applications. His lectures cover many good examples. However, the pacing is extremely fast. So, pre lecture reading is definitely recommended. He is extremely helpful during office hours for homework and test preparations.
Good course on advanced fluid mechanics. Lots of stuff on the lecture to be provided. Very useful in understanding fluids. Exams totally based on the homework.

Spring 2017 was his first time teaching ME 309, highly recommended. Very good professor who is enthusiastic about fluid dynamics. Makes the class exciting by showing practical applications of the subject.  He holds office hours in 1030 after lecture so very accessible for help.  Lastly his lectures mostly include examples which are very helpful!
Overall, it was a great experience. Every class was recorded and the class project was interesting. The instructor and the TA are quite responsive on piazza.
The course is organized well and labs are helpful. Exams are harder than expected though. If you don't study the scheduling algos well you're gonna have a bad time on the test.

I really like the labs in this course because they let me build real embedded systems on ESP32. They are very interesting, and the skills I learned from them are useful. I think the difficulty level of the labs is moderate, and the instructions are really helpful, so it's easy to get started and make progress.

Well-designed course. The lectures are easy to follow, and the lab workload feels fair. The best part is the self-proposed project—you get to come up with your own idea and present it at the end. The only downside is that the project groups are pretty big, so sometimes not everyone contributes equally but still gets the same grade.

The lab assignments are very well designed, and I've learned a ton from them. My favorite part was the final project, where we got the chance to come up with our own ideas, build prototypes, and share our results with the class. It was also super inspiring to see all the fantastic projects from the other teams.

Easiest class at Purdue, ECE362 is way harder. Lectures cover some important embedded system content, but the labs lacked in related content. Wish there were way more using more sensors. Course project ~60% of grade, but received no grade nor feedback. No rubric for project, entirely graded on vibes. This class offered no value to my education.

Lectures were informational and gave a good overview of everything involved with embedded systems. There were only 4 labs and a group project that was heavily weighted because of no exams. The labs had ambiguous instructions, and eventually tutorials by the TA we're given and would have been better if had happen earlier when the labs were assigned.

The homework assignments and practice exams were entirely unrelated to embedded systems, on topics such as Dijkstra's algorithm and ALOHA, which should be taught in other courses such as algorithms and networks, not embedded systems. It felt like the professor decided to teach what he wanted to rather than teach a course on embedded systems.

This was one of the worst courses I have taken at Purdue, due to how disorganized it was. Prof. Su spent many lectures (about half) on random topics such as the internet, and neglected important embedded systems topics. He did not inform us how many labs/homeworks/exams he was planning until early November.

Gotta be the worst ECE professor at Purdue. Unfair class structure with 60% coding labs, 40% cumulative final (barely any practice problems given). Awful lectures - read off slides, low attendance. Professor late to every lecture, completely uninvested in teaching. Final unrelated to labs, included topics he claimed unimportant. AVOID at all costs!
Overall was good, follow the syllabus
Overall, the experience was good
to get A, just follow the course syllabus. She responds with feedback . I experience is positive. My friends got A as well
Great Professor, I recommend. If you follow the syllabus, you will get A
Not that involved in the class at all, but gave me some extra points just by politely asking so I couldn't give a 1/5

This class was a joke, had nothing to do with my major but whatever. Never interacted with professors, online course only. Class had a ton of assignments and readings. Not hard concepts, quit reading and trying two weeks in because it was pointless. Some assignments were super confusing and hard.

Terrible professor. Zero value added to the course. A high school student with a little enthusiasm could have done a better job. Course itself is OK. Just don't expect any help if this professor is still teaching it.
Strict and intimidating

Her ratemyprof reviews are way outdated/have considerably changed. She's genuinely one of the most caring professors I've come across. She's fair, exams are not bad, her slides show exactly which learning objectives they go towards and thus make great study content (some exam questions are directly from the slides).
The exams are were very difficult for the amount that was taught in class. Did not feel like you were ever prepared for the exams.
Over hated, her lectures are good and the course material is standardized so people are getting mad at her for it.
The reason there are so many 1 stars is that the exams are really difficult. She is a decent lecturer and a very nice person, but the exams she makes are ridiculously hard.

Marybeth honestly isn't even that bad of a lecturer she goes over examples and stuff in lecture I think most people give her a bad rating because the course itself is difficult (which it is chem115 is kinda bs sometimes) but honestly Marybeth herself is an alright lecturer. That final was way easier than any of the midterms too.
Just wow, take it at ivy tech or literally anywhere else. Orgo is much better.

Professor Miller is good at describing concepts, and provides good examples during lectures. However, some assignments were difficult with the material not being covered in lecture until the day before it was due and a couple of materials for exams were not very helpful. Overall, a good lecturer but study early for the exams.

She was the course coordinator, and completely changed the course this year. This caused all of the past exam questions and past resources to be unusable and you had to use the textbook problems to actually get a good grade.
Make sure to study even of you think you know it study it again. Get a tutor if you think you need one. This course is difficult.
If you think you need a tutor for this course get one. Chem 115 is not for the weak.

Honestly, I didn't hear great things but in my opinion truly this is just a general chem course. You will have to study and you will have to put time and effort in. She is very clear about what is on the exams. They are also very generous with curves and drops. I feel like the course was very fair and matched my AP chem class pretty closely.

Let me leave it at this. I talked to a friend who had a bf in the course and all she had to say was that he hates it and she never gets to see him even on the weekends even though they both go to purdue. Yk why?? Bc thats how much he and everyone else has to study to try to pass this impossible course. I was also in the course.. dont.

This is the worst course I have EVER taken.She was either really nice or just outright rude, no inbetween. Be ready for 25 question long assignments with each question having 4+ parts even during dead week. The tests are so bad, prepare to study for DAYS straight and still get a 60% bc what she gives you to study isnt even accurate to the tests.

The content she goes over in class is useful and her slides and in-class examples are very well organized. That being said, I would've preferred if she went slower in class since it was next to impossible to take notes. Because of this, I just went to lectures for iClicker and learned by reviewing her slides after, which worked well for me.
I loved listening to her lectures! Very engaging and did a great job explaining complex concepts! There were plenty of opportunities for extra credit by answering iClicker questions.

Class is not particularly difficult with exams covering what is outlined in the learning objectives. I came in with very little chemistry knowledge and did not have difficulty grasping material. Exam questions are NOT strictly from practice exams and understanding of material is required. I would consider her lectures the best of the 115 lectures.

Appreciative of teaching style! She implements many ways to visualize/understand concepts in lecture, works out problems step by step, and posts lecture slides, iclicker question answers, and worked-out practice questions online. Clearly cares about everyones learning and understanding of what is happening and is super friendly! Course is tough!
She is the sweetest ever, she's just a tough grader and the courseload is heavy
Impossible class

She is so underrated. You can tell she really cares about chemistry and loves what she teaches. The exams are hard but you just have to lock in and start studying super early. Miller seems scary but she gives every resource to succeed and the people who complain about her just can't handle an actual college-level class. Best lecturer/coordinator!!

Oh my god this class was the worst experience of my life. Left right after class and refused to let anyone ask any questions, was completely disengaged in office hours. 

0 TA accountability with insanely difficult grading standards, extremely low curving, temper. Absolute NIGHTMARE of a professor. My grades got better after not going to class.

She is given a bad rep from freshman who are not used to actual college leveled classes. She gives a LOT of chances to succeed, ICLICKER extra credit, in class practice problems, LOTS of worst score DROPS, and learning objectives to guide you. I am a freshman and definitely struggled in this class but if you put in the effort you'll do good!!

While CHM115 is definitely a hard course, especially for freshmen, Miller is a good professor. She regularly goes over practice problems in class and gives plenty of examples for reference. Although the exams can be rough, she gives you adequate resources to prepare yourself. She is personable and is willing to get to know you as an individual.
The lectures are long, but she explains everything very well. Also tells you what to study for the tests and if you actually study it you should do very well.
This professor absolutely treats students differently. That's not fair.
This professor is very rude and dismissive of others who disagree with him, as another one the reviews indicates.

This course was supposed to be a speech course, but he decided that the final exam should be written?  Made absolutely zero sense. Gave us 3 min in class to prep a 3 min speech answering a question that you didn't know until you got to class about books that were written in the 1800s translated from German. STAY AWAY

Gives harsh feedback that does not match any rubric. 
Does not consider evidence before disagreeing with students and colleagues in harsh manner. 
Cuts off and talks over women, including graduate students and other professors. 
Generally does not show much regard for the well-being and learning of students. 
I recommend you avoid this professor.

Chris Yeomans is an excellent professor. He's passionate about the material and he makes the lectures engaging. Although he challenges us, it's only to help us be more comfortable with public speaking and discussing complicated texts.

If you want an easy A, get out of this class and stop considering law school, which you may very well be considering if in this class. Otherwise, be ready to read and work hard to understand. An A is very doable if you apply yourself and attend the class. He is a truly great professor with a lot to offer.

Professor Yeomans is one of the best professors at Purdue University. He is SUPER passionate about his subject. But he's not going to hand you an A - you've got to work for it. It's simple - attend every class, do the readings, be prepared - and you'll get an A. If you mess around and don't go to class you're not going to get above a C.
he is absolutely a hard grader.......

It wasn't the hardest class I've taken, but it certainly wasn't easy. His grading was pretty tough, and if you missed much of anything, there was no going back. The subject matter was also pretty ephemeral. All in all, I wouldn't recommend this professor.

Yeoman's is a very difficult teacher.  He is very helpful, but his exams are really hard.  Do not take the honors section of the class.  He is a very hard grader, and from what I understood, in the regular class, the TA's grade.  I would not recommend the class.

All-in-all the professor knows what he is talking about, and will help when asked, but the problem is he is so knowledgeable that you can't understand him too begin with, but I'm pretty sure he curves his classes. If you study with partners you can get through his class fine.
There are very few grades. Even the participation excercises are tough. Do not reccommend this teacher.

Professor is very knowledgeable on the subject but talks much too fast and with a lack of notes to help keep up it is very difficult to understand material. Daily quizzes are given to help keep attendance. Books are incomprehensible. This class is much too difficult as a "beginners ethics", do not take unless you have prior knowledge.

This class was probably my most demanding class. Like the other review said he did teach the course at almost a graduate level so it was sometimes hard to follow him in lecture. The readings can be complicated at times.
Grading makes it almost impossible to do well. If you do bad on one exam there is no making it up. TA's don't know what they are talking about either.

Yeomans teaches this class like a graduate level instead of 100-level.  The material seems comphrehensible until we receive our grades and realize that we must have done something wrong.  This class is not worth taking whatsoever.  It's only going to hurt your GPA... and that's not worth it for an honors class.

He kinda just reads off his slides for lecture (and not very clearly). would not recommend being in his 7:30 AM lecture... you should be alert enough to pay decently close attention if you're in his class.

The problem with this class isnt the professor, its the way its designed. its a trap induced to fail students. i dont blame jung for making this class hard but his attitude towards us at the end was horrible. he didnt respond to emails when we asked for our grades/grade boundaries (finals) and i still dont know my final exam score so thats that

Prof. Jung's lectures were engaging and he explained the most conceptually difficult parts of 2k2 well. His dad jokes were some of the best moments of an otherwise tough class. The exams this semester were brutal, but not written by him, and he did his best to prepare us for them.

Professor Jung's lectures were incredibly hard to follow and were not very well organized in terms of content. The textbook for the class was way more helpful his lectures. No homeworks in the class, just 2 exams and 2 design project submissions. All learning happened by reading textbook, searching for things online, or talking with classmates.

Byunghoo is the best professor to take ECE 2k2 with. His lectures are great and he makes sure to keep students engaged with jokes during the lecture. His exams are managable and he provides lots of great hints during lecture about what he might test on.

This is actually rating for ECE20002. Lectures more complicated than needs to be, much material not covered on HW & exam; can also move fast at times and was still behind schedule. HW more complex than what was on exam, but TAs will help. Exams were mostly fair, but little in class practice or review. Overall, ok; just review material periodically.

ECE 201 is a hard course but Jung makes the best of it. He puts a lot of work into his lectures and tries to ensure that students understand the concepts. Homeworks are useless for exam practice (much more time-intensive than actual exam problems). Focus on doing past exams and you will do well.
Kinda a thick accent. Course in itself is really difficult, and he does an okay job of explaining such things.

This is a tough class, but Jung is a great professor to learn from. He does a great job explaining things in simple terms and emphasizing key concepts. Go to class because the book is extremely unorganized and poorly written garbage.
All my respect to the professor. She is very caring, explains concepts clearly, very sharp, gets straight to the point when answering questions.
The material is common sense and definitely isn't at a university level. The lectures are very unhelpful, but attendance is graded.

This class was very difficult for someone with no prior coding experience. Additionally, I struggled to understand Professor Zhou's accent and wasn't always familiar with the programming terms she used to explain concepts. The exams for this course are designed to trick you, and it's important to get as many points as you can from homework and lab.

Zhou has a pretty strong accent and that can make her pretty difficult to understand.
