great professor who clearly cares about teaching. be sure to ask TAs for help on labs.

Really really helpful and great one on one. He really wants you to learn and grind which is understandable, but gives no lee way what so ever. 344 is a grind class. 242 isnt as much but still challenging for people just getting into things. He is a nice person but a very cut throat professor. Will make you learn whether you like it or not.

Deadman is one of the best professors in the CIT/Cyber program. He is extremely knowledgeable and if you pay attention and put in the effort you'll learn a lot. My only compliant is he treats his class as if it should be your first priority. You want to go home early like Monday for Thanksgiving break expect to miss one of like 4 pop quizzes.
Tough but incredibly valuable professor. Classes are tough, but Deadman's classes are the ones I have gained the most from throughout my experience at Purdue.

This class has a wild reputation, but it's honestly not that bad if you listen to the repeated advice everyone gives. Having a good group is huge. The TAs are pretty active online, so ask lots of questions because they can be really helpful. Biggest tip: don't procrastinate, it will absolutely catch up to you.

Deadman has a reputation as a difficult professor that is well-warranted. He is not a bad guy though, so talk to him and he will address your concerns. Pick a good lab partner who won't flake on you, and show up to lecture. Don't try to get people to sign the attendance sheet for you or put your name on in-class assignments, it won't work.
wasnt there for half the semester but still called me out for chatting during class

content was interesting but, the labs take hours, like 20+ hours. windows based so sometimes you get errors that aren't even your fault and you spent hours troubleshooting. GET A GOOD TA. go into the class knowing who your lab partners are bc that could make or break your grade. he gave us a test the day before thanksgiving break which was annoying

you need a good group for this class so go into it with people yk thats the most important thing otherwise ur cooked. labs take up a looooootttttttttt of time and what makes this class so hard is that there are random errors w windows that you cannot control so it takes hours to troubleshoot. also you need a good ta bc fade some of these tas .
This course is engineered to be harder than it should be in many way but for some reason this dude has a lot of d riders
the type of guy to give an exam the last day before break 👎

The TAs are generally unhelpful and often don't respond to questions at all. Grading is extremely strict, which would be fine if real guidelines were provided. Expectations also vary between TAs, which is frustrating, especially when your own TA is unresponsive and others can't be contacted.

If you get groupmates that are clearly struggling on Lab 1, drop the class and replace it with another class. The full workload for this class is about 40+ hours a week for a 4 credit hour class. This is a recurring problem that I know Deadman is aware of, but he doesn't change the class structure.
Disorganized course. Hardly any effective guidance given.

Assigned an Exam during between the weekend and thanksgiving break. Prevented students from leaving early. Robert explains the material well and is kind towards students. Saved me from a fire once, love that guy.

The labs are VERY time consuming. If you dedicate time for the labs you learn a lot. A lot of time is spent in the lab outside of the actual lab section but Deadman's expectations seem to be reasonable given what you learn.

Professor Deadman is a great professor. Always willing to answer lab or lecture questions during office hours and very approachable. The class is difficult but very much doable. If you put effort into the class you'll see results. My advice to you is to get good lab partners are set a WEEKLY time to meet so you can make progress on the labs.

The course itself is hard but he does a great job of lecturing, way better than Salam. Attendance is taken by signing your name on a sheet and there are 5-6 pop quizzes throughout the semester. Overall great at teaching but will rarely show sympathy for his students. Pick a good lab partner; you cannot switch or drop them "under any circumstances".

A small group of us were branch campus that were forced onto Purdue Main's classes online. Deadman sent a TA out to our campus that offered no help, and made the exam prep in person only (we sent one of ours to WL for this and was met with a rushed briefing that he couldn't take pictures of). He did try to help us, but not enough to keep any of us.

If you study and actually devote yourself in to the class, this is the class and the professor who will teach you A LOT! if you still can't pass after 200% of devotion to the class, at that point take the L Womp Womp...

I do not wanna take his class again but I learned so much. Great professor and very knowledgeable. His class demands that you work hard, but the topics are very relevant and helped me a lot for interviews.

Professor Deadman is the goat. He's passionate about teaching and genuinely cares for his students. Don't get me wrong—CNIT 24200 was very time-consuming and the hardest of the CNIT 200-level courses, but I left the class with a strong, fundamental understanding of systems administration.

This class is super difficult. The material is difficult, the grading is stringent, and it requires A LOT of effort just to get a B. That being said, Deadman is a good guy and very helpful, but the class will humble many students. Get a good lab group and you will be fine in the class. Also, make sure to memorize slides for exams.

The type of teacher to drop a quiz during lecture knowing in advance that many people in the class would not be there watching a total solar eclipse. Very passionate for education though. You will suffer, but at least he is clear on what he expects. His TA's are hit or miss.
So far, the best professor I have had at this institution, and it's not really close. His classes require a lot of effort, but will teach you more than any others like them. Pick good lab partner(s).

He's a great teacher and a great person. He makes time to see his students outside of class to get to know them and he's hilarious in his lectures. He's very organized as a professor and makes all his lecture notes accessible.

His lectures are hard to understand and requires (essentially) re-learning the content outside of class.
Wouldn't take again.

Genuine top tier professor. He clearly cares about his students and his class. Really cool guy off the clock too. He always invites his students to eat lunch with him and talk to him whenever they see him around campus. Fast teacher for sure, but he covers his base. If you pay attention, you're chilling.
Really good professor and super nice.

Best professor ever. He truly cares about his students and values their time. He makes himself available outside of class. In class he cracks random jokes, then awkwardly points it out when no one laughs. When you laugh, he keeps awkwardly going and stretching it, he kills me. He also rounded my grade up by 3%. Such a kind and genuine man overall!
Didn't take attendance but his lectures were clear and concise. The best part is how willing he is to spend time after lectures or outside of class to discuss.
very humble and caring professor , is there for the students and always clears doubts happily . Some might find the course in the second half harder but he will make sure everyone goes along with him
Awesome guy
He did not take attendance for CHM115 but he does have attendance for CHM116. But overall amazing professor.

Genuinely one of the best professors/teachers I've had. He cares for all of his students and makes sure that everyone can understand and apply the material taught in the course. His dedication to making sure everyone succeeds is unmatched, and I wish that I could have him as a professor again.

Genuinely an amazing, respectful person with a heart for his students. One of the best teachers I've ever had in my life. I felt welcomed into university knowing I could walk down the steps after lecture, say hello, and shake his hand. Although I don't have him anymore, I'm going to try to meet him for faculty lunches.Course wasn't too hard either.

He cares a lot for your success and proves it by the amount of time he pours into his students. He offers study sessions prior to each exam and is willing to communicate with his student directly instead of going through a TA. His exams are pretty easy and he provides plenty of study material to help you do well.

A very good prof who prepares his students well for their exams. Cares for each and every student and is willing to answer any questions during lectures. Probably one of the most compassionate professors I've met.

Had Dr. Budhi for ochem. Fantastic guy who really cares about his students success. When he saw that our test average fell he felt as if he failed us as a teacher. While he does have an accent, its not hard too adjust to it. He posts every lecture online and office hours are incredibly accessible and helpful.
Pretty good guy. Does study sessions before each exam and records them. The sessions make the exams more doable. 10/10
Dr. Budhi is a very good professor. He is very knowledgeable and makes the material interesting. He is very accessible during office hours and is willing to help students who are struggling.

Dr. Budhi is a kind, helpful, and approachable CHM 11510 professor. He chats with students before class and around campus. Though some struggle with his accent, he posts lecture slides and recordings. Tests reflect class concepts, and he avoids spoonfeeding answers. Despite teaching >400 students, he tries to get to know each one. Great guy, 10/10!

Professor Daniels will go above and beyond for students that attend office hours and ask questions that reflect any real interest in the course. He will approach the topics from several angles if one is really stuck and uses his breadth of knowledge to help students effectively. The immense impact of office hours cannot be understated!
I had MA 266 with him. Amazing lectures. Super engaging. Even offered to write recommendations for students.
Awesome professor, cares about his students and their understanding of the material. Class is well paced and easy to follow, also holds lots of review sessions and office hours.

Daniels the goat fr. He does a great job explaining the difficult concepts and goes above and beyond to make sure students understand(posting extra notes and examples, clarifying previous notes when there's confusion). Loves when students go to his office hours and takes genuine interest in students outside of class passions. Stay Juiced T Dan 😎🐐

Daniels might be in goat contention. Lectures are slow, but not boring. Very deliberate about all work. Explains everything in detail. Has great banter with students. Will send extra material to the entire class if even one person brings up a difficult topic. Won't let you leave office hours without understanding everything.
Taylor is extremely easy going and explains the content well. I had never taken calc before and was still able to do well.
Talks very fast and doesn't explain what he's doing when showing example problems.

Prof. Bejarano is an amazing 251 lecturer who communicates very well and uses great examples. I think his class is conceptual but helps a lot for the real world. Only downside is the tests aren't always a great reflection of our knowledge. That said, he's pretty fair overall and despite no required attendance, lectures are quite full. Great prof
For CS176. He's smart and has a good sense of humor. But most of the time the lectures aren't that engaging. The notes/online resources weren't managed well, the labs weren't that useful either.
his lectures are optional but they're amazing, i was able to easily follow along with what was happening. it's the only optional class i actually go to.

Provides a exam format that reinforces learning (10 checkpoints instead of 2 midterms). Writes clear to follow and interesting notes in class, and is sociable. Projects can be a bit confusing, but seems to be a consequence of the course, not the professor.

Professor Bejarano made CS25100 engaging with humor and care. He simplifies complex DSA topics while offering invaluable career advice for the real world. More than a teacher, he is a mentor who fosters open discussion and genuinely invests in student success. His support and expertise make him an exceptional, must-take instructor.

Great communicator and teacher, very funny. Very demanding of students but for a tough class, it means we are up to date on the material. Cares enough to answer questions patiently and brings complicated ideas like data structures to life with well explained diagrams. The subject material is by nature difficult but he makes the most of it.

Very test heavy. While taking the class, it felt like I was only studying for 251. 
Prof. tries real hard to be funny, but is not. Lecture notes are exacerbated by poor handwriting. 
Minimal effort given in class, but expects students to ace in biweekly checkpoints. 
Save yourself, take the class with some other Prof.
Any single question you ask in ed or in real life, he responds with it's “in the syllabus”

Prof. Bejerano is good at explaining concepts, but the lecture style involves handwritten notes that can be difficult to follow. The exams are challenging and test very specific details. Because the short, in-class exams are weighted heavily, there is little room for error. The homeworks are long but have low impact on the overall grade.

He definitely knew his stuff, but he just sucked at explaining things. The classes did not have any slides, and did not follow a textbook, so keeping up outside of the lectures was difficult. On top of that, lectures felt like they don't slowly build your knowledge, but instead just throw material at you. Finally, the "notes" were just unreadable.

Easily one of my favorite CS professors I've had the fortune of learning from in my time here. He is clearly well versed not only in the material, but also how to appropriately teach it. Some of the best paced and digestible lectures I've ever attended. I think he's witty,charming, and clearly cares about his students. Can't recommend him enough!

A very good professor, explained everything clearly and also had some funny jokes. Sarcastic in brightspace, and in a cool way. Tests were difficult, but fair. He set a decently big curve for the class.

I wish I delayed taking this class with another professor; the checkpoints- which are nearly 50% of your grade don't correspond to h.w's or what was necessarily taught in class - didn't cover material in lecture? Good luck, it might show up on your exam the next day, all the while trying to juggle a ton of other assignments.
Lots of tests that are weighted relatively heavily with a lot of busy work that amounts to nearly nothing.

Some of the best lectures I have ever attended.
Class is heavily skewed toward tests.
Tests are difficult but they are fairly graded.
Felt that lectures were kinda boring but at least were informative. TAs were usually very harsh and grading things wrong. Had to submit many regrade requests to argue my points.

If you're going to take 251, try to do it with him. He's one of the most competent lecturers in the department and teaches the content really well. Homeworks and projects are tough, so make sure to reach out to TAs.

By far the best lecturer I have ever had. He writes down essential information and elaborates on it during lectures. I think the clearest way to tell he was great was the fact that attendance wasn't required but the lecture was always packed. My only problem is that the TAs were pretty useless and gave wrong information sometimes.
GOAT lecturer and fair grading.

Definitely go to all lectures and take notes. Posada is an AMAZING lecturer and really makes otherwise difficult concepts easy to understand. Midterms and the final are worth most of your grade, so be sure to study ahead of time. Also complete the projects, they will boost your understanding of concepts even through they are hard.
Very good lecturer. Class was not too hard, homeworks and projects were doable if you started early.

lectures are good but he usually explains easy examples while the homeworks and quizzes have more complicated ones, important information like test format was not provided and feedback on midterms was not very accessible as well. However, the class is not too hard overall.

One of my favorite CS professors I've had at Purdue, his lectures were extremely helpful. Not mandatory, but I highly recommend going to them, they're pretty easy to follow and take notes on. Lots of examples so that we can see the concepts in practice. The content is definitely hard, the projects took a while, and there were 3 midterms.

Very hard class with a lot of workload but the professor was quite easily the best CS professor I have had at Purdue. The lectures are optional but he does an amazing job of explaining every concept and makes them very interesting.
My favorite CS professor by far. His lectures are amazing and he clearly explains the difficult concpets well. His exams and projects can be difficult, but far from unreasonable.
Very good professor, great notes and posts lectures online. Beware of exams, they are 25% each and there are 3.

She is extremely passionate about her subject material but she doesnt teach it well. she starts going off on random tangents mid class. also, all her lecture notes have blanks in them which you need to fill IN CLASS so if you miss a day, you need to watch a 1.5 hour recording of her yapping to be caught up so you can study for her exams.

I really don't like her style of notes just loooong slideshows each week of just fill in the blank text and her exams are actually kinda tricky even if you study but you should do fine. Little bit of extra credit. Watch out tho as exams make up 75% of your grade.

shes taking over for previous prof. recorded lectures and chapter reading. lots of concepts do not fall behind. get comfortable with stata and learning on your own. tricky wording on the homework questions so really know the concepts in and out. get good grades in the homework and discussions and you'll be fine. I learned a lot in the class
Base level material is supposed to be learned outside the classroom. Lectures are long and complex without space for grasping the concepts in class. Exams are very heavily weighted.

Lectures consisted of 50 slides making everything very confusing. Fill in the blank notes with long tangents made understanding the material difficult. I also had to use outside resources to learn the material. She seems very knowledgeable but class is very poorly taught.

Lectures were disorganized and hard to follow, making simple concepts confusing. Fill-in-the-blank slides and long explanations didn't help with understanding. I had to rely on outside resources to learn the material. She would talk about stories that had zero correlation to the topics. The issue wasn't the subject, but how poorly it was taught.

She is really good at relating to the students during lecture and cares about their success. While she may not explain things in a way that is understandable for everyone and the slides can be word-heavy, the practice exams are helpful and the class is not too difficult.

She is really good at relating to the students during lecture and definitely cares about their success. While she may not explain things in a way that is understandable for everyone and the slides can be a lot, the practice exams are helpful and the class is not too difficult.
Pretty funny teacher, lectures move pretty fast so some concepts didn't stick for me.

Dr. Banan was an excellent professor! Overall, a very manageable class with a decent amount of extra credit offered and many free points from iClicker and homework. Exams can be difficult, but they are very similar to the practice exams provided.

Banan is an excellent professor. Her course material is very well organized on Brightspace, her lectures are easy to follow and well paced, and the lecture notes she uploads after class are high quality. The exams are straightforward, as long as you prepare well, you shouldn't have any problems. There is also a good amount of extra credit available

Professor Banan was a good professor for ECON 251. The last 2 exams were difficult, but if you study a lot and really understand the notes she provides, you'll do fine! She gives a lot of extra credit, so make sure to take advantage of all of it early in the semester. I suggest going to every class and don't fall behind on notes.

75% of the grade are exams. tough exams i cant lie. read the textbook, went to every lecture, reviewed notes, (tl;dr i would spend hours on hours to study and not get above a B). she's a good professor overall though - fun lectures, sometimes can get a bit boring (content/not her). she's also always available. very hard class, great professor.

She will care as much as you do. She's competent and witty. Her lectures and exams are content heavy. It's better to develop an intuitive understanding of the material, rather than trying to memorize rules. Read your syllabus before emailing her. The answer is usually in there already, and you don't want to be that person when emailing a professor.

Great teacher, classes are super helpful and videos are great for learning. Homework is super easy and exams aren't insanely hard either.
Shoutout all the TAs as well for helping with exams and difficulties too!

Dr. Banan is clearly very knowledgeable about her field, but I'm not sure how she has such high ratings. Her lectures are slow, boring, and her cadence quite monotonous. She also comes off as quite condescending and rude sometimes, feels like she has a massive ego. But 251 is a pretty easy class and she does give resources to do well on exams.

Dr. Banan is a great professor, her content is well organized and she offers plenty of resources to study and review. ECON251 is well organized and her system for teaching, grading, and homework is well refined.

Dr. Banan was an amazing lecturer, and I found the course content easily digestible through her examples and explanations. Microeconomics can sometimes be hard to understand, but she always made sure to answer each question thouroughly and elaborate on topics when needed.

I enjoyed Dr. Banan this semester. Econ is not my favorite class, but her lectures were engaging and funny. The homework does not feel like a waste of time. Just study and pay attention most of the time, and you'll be fine. Don't go to the TA review sessions-- they are useless. The SI review sessions are better.
She sucks,

Prof Banan clearly knows her stuff and cares. Sometimes she can let little things bother her and let that disrupt class. She also sometimes gives long stories as examples of stuff which bothers some people but I think it makes lectures more interesting. Homework can be tricky (what textbook is required for). Attendance only mandatory for half sem.

Grades are 75% exams. She is a sweet and understanding person if you get the chance to talk 1-on-1. Very smart while also very human. Her lectures are pretty engaging and easy to understand. She's a really good prof imo. Just study and you'll be ok. Don't stress too bad. She also has extra credit. Very fair class in all. Go to office hours trust
She was sometimes condescending. But everything else is good. Her lectures were fun, and she taught well.
Dr. Banan is a great professor. Overall, I struggled with Econ but she's really great if you go to her office hours. Lectures can be a bit dry and exams are slightly difficult. Not bad overall.

This class has a reputation for being the hardest class you take as a Purdue IE, and I 100% agree. This class is designed to make you uncomfortable because that's how the real world is. While it may seem hopeless during the semester. If you work hard, you will pass. Be attentive and upfront.

He cares about his students, but the class itself is just a ton of work. He's super understanding, and I learned a lot from 332. The only negative thing I have to say is that he teaches the lecture content (how computers work) as if we already understand the terms and vocab he's using, so it's hard to follow along sometimes.

Purdue needs more courses like this. Mario's an excellent lecturer. Content is very difficult but anybody else teaching it would only make it worse. Course is closest thing you'll get to "work experience" during Purdue. Difficult but rewarding. 

Go to lectures, ask questions. He's great at answering questions and he makes lectures fascinating.

Mario is a great dude. Lectures are very interesting and cover a TON of information. The class is notoriously hard because of the big project-based assignments. The class is very do-able and 0 people failed the class when I took it. Get with your group early and get on the same page about how assignments will be divided.

Its a very difficult class but one that teaches you a lot. Mario is a great lecturer and reviews real world and relevant content. The assignments are verry difficult, graded hard and time consuming but teach you a lot in the process. The course has definitely improved compared to past years and although you'll have to work hard its a good class

Time commitment wasn't actually too bad as I had feared, but certainly difficult.  Homeworks are tough but graded fairly, and you'll figure out what to study for quizzes. Mario is an excellent lecturer, though sometimes less pleasant on Piazza, No time in this class is a waste--go to every lecture and never slack off. I enjoyed it quite a bit.

He is a good professor, but the material is tough. The class is far more like the real world than most students are prepared for, but the class teaches you a lot about handling timelines on engineering-level work.

The grading is very hard. Will take off points for entire questions, even if only a small part of the answer is incorrect. Goes out of his way to provide the least amount of help possible. Grades everything late. The quizzes are packed with so much content that it is impossible to completely study for. Overall, this class is designed to screw you.
He is bad.

You interface with the TAs more then Mario. Class is famously hard, but you learn a lot. I don't like that lectures are online and class time is just for questions. Know code syntax for the practicums. Start early and motivate your teams for the projects, they are a lot of work. Expect 20+ hour weeks for the projects for most of the semester.

This will be the hardest class to take as a IE. I would block out whichever semester to take this class in. But you learn a ton of valuable information and skills to use in real life. Unfortunately, Mario does not do the best job helping students with the bare minimum at times. Be ready to research and communicate with your group.

Professional, knows the material, wants best for student success. Quizzes are hard, & feedback was not provided until well into semester. Projects & assignments are quite time consuming with little instruction. If you ask questions on piazza be prepared to receive a condescending answer. Make sure to ask questions in lecture, as he is helpful then.

332 is all about a simulated real world experience with the group assignments and projects. This class had our group working upwards of 40 hrs/week on the project alone. Mario is not helpful at all, he will be intentionally unhelpful via Piazza, the lecture videos are at least 4 years old and sometimes you have to watch like 6 of them. It sucks

Mario is very intelligent and a great lecturer. He really wants students to learn. But he does not care about their academic success. The projects are very vague, heavy work, and graded harshly. He teaches a lot of interesting useful topics, but makes the class harder than it needs to be.

There is no doubting that he is smart and knowledgeable but he is not a good professor. He does not teach the class well and makes the quizzes very complicated. The labs only touch on the basics of R but students are expected to learn complex R code and spend hours on the projects. Oh and don't bother asking questions in Piazza, he'll just be rude.

IE 332 is easily the hardest class in the entire program. Professor Ventresca is a huge reason why. The project, assignments, labs, and tests are challenging. The instructors are never helpful, TAs often do not attend office hours. There is no curve but the professor assigns extra credit to individual students at his discretion.

Teaches interesting material but packs too much information in a short amount of time. Go to office hours and make sure you maintain group communication. Quizzes and exams were doable imo if you keep up with the lecture slides. The labs are just for credit but if I could do it again I'd take them more seriously as it helps for the project.

Professor Ventresca answers questions on Piazza in a condescending manner. Guidelines for labs were rarely clear and questions for clarification were met with disdain. The content is interesting but brutal with the limited guidance we were provided. The lectures provided a minimal explanation for the final project (50% of our grade).

This course is traditionally known as the hardest IE course and that is entirely true. Prof. Ventresca is the most intelligent professor I've had but he is matter-of-fact and makes you think about/work for the answers to your questions. Be prepared to put a LOT of time into this class. It's difficult but certainly not impossible; trust the process.

Please just take your D and get out as fast as you can. This dude does not even answer student emails. He relies on TAs to give vague answers to everything. The project is the hardest thing Ive ever done in college. Rewarding class I guess, but dont expect an amazing grade and dont expect to ever talk to Mario. Ever.

This is the worst course I've had the misfortune of taking during my time at Purdue. The lecture materials do not go nearly far enough in explaining concepts to be successful in the semester project and even large parts of the homework. There are no office hours or help sessions, so you can expect to be on your own most of the semester. Good luck.

Ridiculous. Worst professor ever. He will never give you a straight answer he talks extremely slow and grades to fail students. Almost will never know what exactly is going on since the project has absolutely nothing the do with lecture. Good luck if you think the lectures will help you on the HW.

Mario Ventresca was probably one of the worst professors I have ever had at Purdue. He did not offer office hours this semester which made doing the homework and studying for exams borderline impossible. Additionally, his grading is too harsh; he barely curved the class even though our averages were in the 50s for everything. He is horrible.

This was one of the hardest classes Ive taken but also one of the most rewarding. Mario is an amazing lecturer, and is always open to questions at any time. Furthermore, he will be more than happy to reexplain if you dont understand something. He loves what he teaches. His project is hard, but it help teach me to be the engineer I am today.
Great Professor but the expectations of this course may be too high. Too many different coding languages taught in a short amount of time. The project should be made easier.

Lots of assignments, very easy to miss if not scheduling time. He doesn't have strong lectures, often times you will have to do reading on your own through the textbook. Material isn't really that hard though, can get difficult towards the end.

Very disorganized class. There is a thousand things you get graded on and every single one of them is all over the place. Lectures themselves are ungoable because they are so boring and do not even matter for the exam. The exam is just a random stuff that could have been talked about in the past two months. No study guide or example exam whatsoever
pain lecturer

Do yourself a favor and don't take IE 343 with Lehto. Lehto is possibly the worst lecturer at Purdue, shows no enthusiasm for the class, and is only capable of mumbling off the slides. You're better off skipping lecture and teaching yourself. In terms of content, IE 343 is not a hard class. Pre and post-session quizzes and homework are free points.

I never attended his lectures because they are so boring put me to sleep. Pre and Post class quizzes but just stay on top of it. The HW was not bad. We got crib sheets for each exam and you can do well you just have to learn the material yourself which I dont mind. If you can find an exam from past years from a friend its good looks.

Honestly so frustrating how this class could've been so interesting and had potential to teach so much valuable stuff but it was actually so sh*t. The way that the class is structured just provides zero valuable lessons, the slides and lectures are easily the most boring thing in the world. I didn't even go to class and I would study 3 days before.

He is nice, but he has got to be the most boring, hard to understand, and overall terrible professor. What could be an interesting and valuable class turned into a chore by week 3. Exams are very difficult and hard to prepare for because his slides are absolute nonsense. However grading is lenient. Please do yourself a favor and changes sections.

Prof Lehto himself is really nice and lenient but the class itself isn't to great. He just reads off slides, grading and assignments can be all over the place, however its more busy work then hard work. No study guides or practice exams so make sure you got a good cheat sheet.

Lectures were just nonsense, don't bother going. Exams are over the textbook so you will need to read it regardless, which is quite a dense book to get through. Logistically this course was a mess-late grades, changing exam and homework formats, and lab instructions that made no sense. Clearly no effort is being put in to make this course valuable.

I actually enjoyed IE-343 but it was just taught in the most boring way possible. He seems like a good person, but has a complete inability to lecture engagingly. I went to almost every lecture (there was a total of like 5 others) but I would actually recommend NOT. Read the textbook and prepare to work harder than you would have expected.

IE386 as a whole feels discontinuous and confusing. The content itself is not that hard - it's just a lot of equations - but it is taught in such a boring, disengaging way that it becomes overly difficult. The exams are unrewarding in that they are just cherry-picked from the textbook, and you either know it or you don't...there's no reasoning.

Completely, absolutely useless lectures. The entire class of 75+ skips lectures. Way too many small assignments with due dates that often do not match the dates that we are taught the material. Exams are easy if you study and they grade lightly, but the teaching makes the experience a major 0/10.
Worst experience

Lehto is the worst professor I have ever had. I stopped attending lectures because he genuinely did not teach anything and would rather mumble about obscure material. His lecture slides provide very little explanation on solving problems and are often just random screenshots from the textbook. The pre and post session quizzes are unnecessarily hard

Worst professor I've had during my time at Purdue. Most of the time I was resorting to just using the textbook to complete assignments, quizzes, and study. Exams test a lot of trivial concepts and are unnecessarily tricky, as they have a lot of questions about stuff that isn't stressed in lecture slides. Everything else is easy though.
Boring.  Does not test on what he mumbles about.  Obscure teacher that gives no guidance on anything.  Better to stay home

Each lecture has 50 minutes worth of "Pre-Class Videos" because he can't actually convey meaningful information within a class period. It is an easy course made difficult by a professor with a fundamental ineptitude for teaching in an interesting way. I would find it more engaging listening to an audio reading of the Webster dictionary.

This professor was one of the worst I have had, he is not enthusiastic and just goes through the motions. He has the TAs run the course, deferring all questions to them. The lectures cover a lot of material that could be interesting but he provides very little explanation. A lot of homework and attendance questions came directly from the textbook.
Probably the worst instructor I've ever had in my 22 years of being a student. I've had elementary school teachers that were vastly more helpful than he was. Lazy, boring, etc

NEVER take a class with this professor, he cares very little and lets the GAs run the whole show for him. The lectures cover a TON of material with very little explanation forcing you to read the book he wrote. Also kept changing the formats up of each exam without warning till a few days before. The most boring man in existence.
NEVER TAKE THIS PROFESSOR COURSE, EXAMS ARE TOO BIG, VERY LESS GRADE, NO MARKS COMPENSATION

Prof Lehto seems to not really care about his lectures. The content could be interesting but he is incredibly boring and doesn't teach well enough for the quizzes, homework's, and exams. Most of what you will learn comes directly from the book which he wrote. You probably don't need to go to class because you're better off teaching yourself.
Very boring lectures

I had Mark Lehto for IE386. His lectures were extremely boring and consisted of him standing in the front of the room, while reading from extremely vague powerpoints. He doesn't seem to care much about the course and it feels like he lets the TAs run it for him. He taught a huge variety of IE concepts and went into very little detail about them.

Engineering economics, an otherwise interesting and important topic, became boring and extremely difficult after having Lehto. I would say rely on the textbook to learn the material, but even then the exam time limits are so strict that knowing the content is only half the battle. Try to avoid this professor if you can
Generally a pretty well managed class. Professor genuinely cares about students. Exams 1 & 2 are similar to homework's. Exam 3 is somewhat difficult. Overall nice class.

An insanely smart professor who also has a burning passion for the topic. She will also randomly drop some wisdom about life at the end of a lecture. Content is somewhat difficult at first, but Professor Yih explains things in a way that simplifies it.

Great class and she teaches it very well. Some content is overcomplicated in my opinion but very applicable to the real world. Tests hard but fare. Professor keeps lectures interesting, engaging (will sometimes ramble a little in her story) but overall a good class.

Overall the lectures aren't the "greatest," but it is very hard making Integrated Production Systems interesting. The homeworks are the hardest things you'll have in this class. I studied for the final exam for less than 10 cumulative hours and got nearly 100%. Exams are generally easy. Review sessions with the TAs are not very helpful though.

Not a fan of her teaching style, but I made the most of this course through understanding the HW. The material was really boring, and it may be tedious sometimes. Exams were fair until the final, I had to second guess myself a lot so make sure you do really well on the 1st and 2nd exam.

Prof. Yih is a really great lecturer, even if the course content was not the most exciting. Things felt a bit messy with many cancelled classes and lectures/notes being one continuous stream of info, but everything is posted on Brightspace. Exams are short and weighted heavy so you have to be careful, but the questions are easy to predict and solve

Dr. Yih has been teaching this class for decades, so she knows and teaches the content very well. She really emphasized preparing students for their future jobs, and she also gave general life advice. A great person and great professor.
She clearly enjoys the content she teaches. Very nice person. Make sure to do well with the homeworks, but they're not too bad. Study for the exams.

One of the best professors in the department. Cares a lot about her students and will give life advice outside the topics of the course. Make sure to know the lecture material and homeworks well as that is what the exams are like.

Dr. Yih is a wonderful person and teacher. Only downside is she gets caught in tangents and runs over time in class, making the classes seem like they take forever. Otherwise, she is a great teacher which tries to teach a valuable subject, and much better than IE484 is taught in comparison.
Lecture prepares you for homework. Homework prepares you for exam. Simple as.

If youre religious, this woman is Mother Mary. If youre not, this woman is Betty White, or like Martha Stewart (minus the prison part), or some other American treasure. Amazing teaching and explanations, you can tell she cares that her students learn the material, and will also throw in amazing some life advice during lectures.

Obviously very knowledgeable and caring, but really, really bad lecture notes (disorganized). Theres also no textbook so lectures are all you can rely on. Power hour felt long. Tests are 80% (40% final, 20% each midterm). Lots of HW and quizzes but only worth 20%. Midterms were long for the time given but not hard. Final was MC, also not bad.

She is very knowledgable in manufacturing and she knows what she is talking about. hard working and professional. Aside from classes, she also enlightens people about life and living. She is very helpful outside the classroom giving students advices regarding careers and graduate school as well. She is very impressive!
she is not at all co operative and extremely arrogant. she does not reply to e mails and not at all helpful. her explanation is also not good and clear.
Go to all lectures and be familiar with hwk materials!
Prof. Yih is one of my favorite teachers at Purdue. Go to lecture and she teaches you everything you need to know - no book. Material is interesting too.

She is one of the best professors I have ever had at Purdue.  Even though it was a larger class, she showed she cared about us as people by giving 5 minutes of advice every Thursday at the end of class. Only complaint; she posted a textbook on MyPurdue and then we never needed it.
She is very helpful and makes the class interesting. This is by far the best class and the best professor I've had at Purdue thus far.

484 was ridunckulous.  poor mg, he was ta when i took it.  semester project, parking lot simulation, was jacked up.  she offered little help or any real knowledge for the class.  i think we learned like three things.
Great prof. If you pay attention in class, she will tell you everything you need to know. Gives speeches on life at the end of lectures. Good stuff.
Not helpful to students. Doesn't plan her classes and assignments well.
very arrogant and don't explain well
Wonderfule person and professor.  Enjoys sharing experience.
If you have a good background in IE, this class is not too hard; it's easy to make a mistake but the concepts are not hard to learn.  The exams are work-out problems based on homework and lectures.

I liked Dr. Altman's teaching style, he's straightforward and explained ochem in a digestible way. H cared about his students and made himself accessible to students. The course is mainly exam grades. There is some extra credit, I would recommend going to lecture, doing the extra textbook problems, and going to office hours.
He is not the best at directly addressing questions in a neutral way, so if they help you learn just be ready to accept advice along with snark

MEMORIZE THOSE MECHANISMS! Also his exams when he starts teaching will have one huge question about the last few slides that he teaches and that is what trips up people bc they dont study that part. Have a master sheet for all the mechanisms. skips lots of in-class practice problems so not v helpful. uses iclicker sometimes to check understanding.

Dr. Altman is a very kind guy and always responds to emails. In lecture, he moves very quickly and can skip over important details, but otherwise he is a great lecturer. Always gives a recap of the previous lecture before introducing new content. Exams are pretty fair, but be ready for a lot of memorization. Overall, a pretty solid professor.

Ryan Altman's lectures were difficult to comprehend, and his portions of the exams were even worse.  I definitely think he could've explained things a lot better. He even felt disappointed in us due to our exam scores, but I feel like that could've been avoided if his exams were better. he did well in explaining some difficult mechanisms though.
I love Dr Altman! He is so great and such a wonderful teacher. He makes things easy to understand and is super helpful with things outside of class.

One of my favorite professors I've ever had. He is an incredible lecturer and extremely charismatic. The way he's able to take a complex mechanism and explain it in understandable terms helped me immensely. He also is extremely helpful if you ask a question to him over email. Exams are fair for the content taught. Overall, amazing professor.

Altman was a good guy. I definitely think he could've explained things a lot better. I also was disappointed in the fact he wouldn't post practice problems catered to his exam questions and his response to that was: "There's something called self sufficient studying and Google!" Overall, his lecture style was digestible.

Dr. Altman knows his stuff. He is very clear about what you do and don't need to know. He invites asking a lot of questions, both in and out of lecture. He's a little dry and straight to the point, but overall I liked his lectures- they're efficient.

Dr. Altman clearly cares about the success of his students. He responds to emails quickly and invites questions. He lectured very well and made it apparent what was expected. The lectures were engaging while also straight to the point. I did not use the textbook, but it may be helpful to some people.

The transition from Kern to Altman was rough, considering I learned so well with Dr. Kern. Altman wasn't terrible all-around, he was just relatively boring to listen to. He also did not work out problems fully when he thought that we should remember how to do them. He also had sloppy handwriting and did not explain concepts fully.

Not sure why Altman got poor reviews--I had him this semester for CHM 255 and he is great!! He answers questions in class and office hours and goes through practice problems during lecture. Plus he has a really cute dog and brought her in for the last day of class!

This semester, alternated between Dr Altman and Dr Kern. Both were good lecturers, CHM 255 is just a very hard course with a huge focus on memorization and practice. Be sure to utilize the resources given. Textbook was "required" but also not needed to do the course work.

Dr Altman was great. He was super open to answering questions and made it clear what was expected of us. The practice exams were pretty accurate to what the actual exam was like too which was super helpful. I really enjoyed having him as a professor.

I had both Kern and Altman during my semester in CHM 255 and both are amazing in their own ways. When Altman took over the second half of the semester, I was worried because of how much I had liked Kern but Altman was also a very good/fun professor. He shows that he is passionate about the subject and teaches it well.

I have no words. Ryan was a terrible lecturer and just rude to engage with. He would encourage asking questions and then shame the ones who did. There were no helpful study tools for exams. I like Orgo but he made it very unenjoyable. Do not take it if you can.

He is the most passive aggressive person you will ever meet. He is a terrible professor, and he stated in class that he googles the quiz/exam questions. I should have took Orgo 2 elsewhere- or even had Google teach it to me instead of him. We never knew what to expect on quiz/exams bc he didn't give us any material to help us prepare for them.

The lecture slides were full of content from top to bottom, to the point it was impossible to know what to study for exams. First semester was very difficult, leading to a chunk of our cohort not meeting the exam average. Second semester was a bit better but still a very tough class and professor.
Absolutely brutal.

She is a sweet professor if you reach out to her for help, but content is EXTREMELY hard to memorize and she does not do a good job at all of summarizing material. I think I probably spend 30-40 hours studying per exam and my average is only an 81%. She's better this semester, but last semester 1/3 of our cohort was failing and she did nothing...

She was overall a good professor. She presents a lot of information in lecture, but if you memorize the information, you will do good on the exams. Attendance is mandatory in her class, and she usually does in-class assignments for points. She makes time for questions and is always straightforward with us. I would take again.

Swartzell is great teacher! You have to put the work in or else she won't help you but she clearly wants us to understand. A lot of the people that complain about her didn't put the work in. Would take again. First B I've gotten in my life and I still love her. Hard class but not her fault.
Epitome of nursing students. Does not care about the success of her students.
She had excellent slides that were very informative. Also, she mentions exactly what you need to know for the test. Very good lecturer.

Professor Swartzell was a great professor, but the content learned is very difficult. The class is very fast paced and there isn't time for questions, so you would have to meet with her in office hours. I thought she was the best professor out of the two professors who taught this class, but the content is very difficult. Study the slides!

Worst professor ever. The one comment you see on here with a class grade of A and good quality was clearly written by her because she's compensating for the amount of students who are failing her class. Avoid.

Absolutely terrible professor. 1/3 of the cohort is currently failing. Doesn't care about our grades or anybody in the class. Should not be a professor at this school. If a 1/3 is failing and another chunk is close to failing, the issue is not the students, it's the professor.

People hate on her, but to be fair, she teaches the content, makes good slides, and gives the resources you need to do well. That being said, she is a no-nonsense prof and will not care if you are failing. Either you learn the content and get the grades you earn or you will fail.

I asked her to explain one of her slides and she genuinely told me to ask chatgpt because she was too lazy to explain it to me. I have never gotten a B in my life and I have truly never worked harder for it in this class. She genuinely does not care about you. Be prepared to teach yourself everything!

Literally does not care if you fail even when it means you have to graduate a year late. When you ask for her help she says ,"You can figure it out yourself" or "You can read the textbook". If you have to take a class with her RUN!!! or transfer :)
Very good professor. Really allows you to learn in your own way. I really learned a lot.

Jonathan is so passionate about American history, and it shows in every lecture, discussion, and assignment. He doesn't simply teach dates and facts; he teaches the stories behind events, the human experiences, and the connections that link the past to the present. If u enjoy learning history in a way that feels alive and relevant, take his class.

This professor is super passionate about history. I did it as an online 8 week course and it was a little busy to try and fit all the content in. I enjoyed the lectures and he gives you the textbooks for free. The grades are for discussion posts, reading quizzes, and three big essays.

Professor McFall clearly knows the material, and is always willing to answer students' questions. Tends to ramble on in lecture unnecessarily long, which can be frustrating for a primarily studio class. Grades mostly fairly and accepts regrade requests respectfully, but the class itself contains an excessive amount of work, often very difficult.

L course, dull lecturer. Hazy hw grading criteria, no learning is accomplished. He yaps for eternity so dozing off may occur. Attendance is required so you can only miss a few classes. Incessant work and questions answered with a "looked down upon" attitude.

His stuff dont really make sense, he insisted in his grading scale on his slides that contradicts the syllabus, and the course itself its poorly structured. He also talk about non-related stuff a lot which makes it hard to pay attention.

McFall is knowledgeable in the content he teaches, but he often gets sidetracked and talks for a long time about unrelated topics, taking up a lot of the work time students are supposed to have each class. Also, he does very little about unbalanced effort in groups, even when it is brought to his attention in a respectful manner multiple times.

One of the most inspirational professors at purdue, really helps you feel like you belong as an engineer. His teaching style is good, and he is amazing at answering questions. Lots of stories in class, and relating what you learn in ENGR 130 to industry experiences. However, Engr 130 is very homework heavy and can feel like busywork at times.

Professor McFall did a pretty good job introducing material but he loves to talk. He spends too much time going over material which usually not needed for the tasks that we have in class. The homework assignments are graded very hard with no rubric to go off of. The group projects come down to how good your group is and if they want to work.

The lectures can be long, but he makes them entertaining with his personal anecdotes. The course, however, is organized in such a way where homeworks and exams are graded with intense scrutiny, yet projects are not. Be aware of this and make sure to double check every detail of information you put on homeworks especially.

He's really not that bad. Sometimes the lectures he gives can drag, but little of what he says is needed to do well (and you can just play on ur phone). Plus he seems to care a lot about making sure women/minorities in the class don't feel excluded. All of my gripes about this class come from the TAs and the curriculum.

He isn't a bad professor by any means. He does have a habit of going on tangents which are only related to the topic half the time. The rubrics on gradescope are quite vague and a lot of people have lost points for seemingly no reason on assignments or tests. Your experience in the class will probably be more dependent on the TA's for the semester.

McFall was an alright professor in ENGR131. The assignments are graded on a rubric inaccessible to students and on an extremely nitpicky basis, meaning you often lose points for things that weren't very clear. His lectures at the start of class are often too long and frequently go off topic, but he is very kind and helpful when answering questions.

He likes to talk way too much at the start of class. You end up missing a lot of "studio time". His lectures aren't very engaging. He is, however, a kind guy and will help you out if there is a problem.

Decent enough person, but terrible professor. Every single lecture was covering material that was already required to learn through the pre-class videos. The first hour of each class was just restating what was in these videos, then expecting students to complete large group assignments in the remaining hour or less.

I'm honestly surprised to see so many negative reviews. To be fair he does talk, a LOT, but there are way worse things for a professor to be than talkative. He seems to care about his students. You're allotted 6 unexcused absences per semester, which is honestly a lot, and there's a ton of extra credit opportunities at the end of the semester.

I think the professor is pretty helpful and funny but his lectures tend to drone on a lot. However, the class itself just isn't super engaging and a lot of slides for lectures. The coursework itself isn't super hard but the grading can take getting used to at first as it is strict for the home works but it prepares you for the exams.

He talks so much. He doesn't care for his students and is very strict at times. The class itself I feel like doesn't depend so much on the professor, but I would take it with a different professor if possible.

He's a great guy, he's funny and genuinely cares about his students. His lectures drone on and on and are incredibly boring, but it's the same set of slides for every teacher. The class itself is basically entirely group work and you're with the same group the whole semester. The grading can be pretty harsh but the guidelines are clear.
grades unfairly, he made fun of a disabled person in my section. Horrendus class take it with literally anyone else
I don't even know what to say just take it and u will understand it.
I had McFall for 132, and he was a very nice person, but the class was really poorly organized. We received ru

130 is a horribly organized course. This might be a course a student would take freshman year of high school. It is all busy work, team building activities, and projects that are entirely useless. Nothing learned in this class will make you a better engineer, besides how to avoid AI detection on coding homeworks. 
The instructor isn't much better.

It is a good class to learn Excel and he does care about his students. Make sure you complete all work and assignments on time and he is very understanding. Gives out extra credit towards the end of the semester. Gave a bad vibe right at the start yelling at us to be quiet but improved throughout the semester.

Mac was a very caring professor and was a very fair grader, as long as you name your files correctly before submitting. He can be hard to listen to, as he talks a lot in class. Good luck if you have him at 7:30. Also ENGR 131 will test you on the amount of effort you are willing to put in. For a 2 credit class, it will push your limits some weeks.

The grading criteria was extremely unclear and resulted in unnecessarily harsh grading despite the content not being particularly difficult. Professor McFall was very unhelpful when it came to clarification of the grading and nobody seemed to know who was in charge of the grading criteria.

The criteria for grading is completely unclear. The TA's are completely clueless about what's going on. We are tasked to do things that we haven't learned. There is no clear guide that we can follow on our assignments or our exams or our group projects.

Really cares about his students! Lectures always run long and are a bit boring but a super nice prof who makes you feel like you can succeed in ENGR 131. A bit condescending when he is trying to get the attention of the entire class, but overall super great!

Professor Yeung did a very adequate job of conveying the material. Lectures gave all important topics and many practice problems, which was quite helpful. Gave extra practice problems before exams and worked on them in lecture. Exams were difficult but reasonable. Lectures mirrored exam content. Yeung did an excellent job of answering questions.
Often messed up example problems and seemed unprepared for lectures.

His lectures were always confusing, as he often made simple algebra mistakes and sometimes would be completely wrong on problems, never fully explaining and saying “you should know this from high school”. Lectures started with all 350ish people and ended with less than 50 coming at the end. Just go to another lecture!

Great lecturer and extremely supportive. Explains concepts clearly and really cares about students' success. Is also extremely understanding about illnesses and making up exams. Sometimes makes small algebra mistakes in lectures, but corrects them quickly. Exams were challenging, but the class was fair, and I felt I learned a lot.

Guy can't lecture I got tired of watching him make algebra errors. Go to the other lecture if you can or just watch Chenflix. Hard class, but you probably can't get around this class anyways, so good luck. Exams are HARD. Nothing written in syllabus prohibiting AI use, but use wisely.
Messes up so much that people don't know what to believe

Overall. great experiences during lectures. I felt very consistent. He organized his lectures to discuss topics from previous classes. While the exams are a bit challenging, it goes the same for most MA courses. Pro tip: consider use Boilerexams before your recitation quizzes and exams (it helps).
he doesn't really know how to keep people's attention, half the class stopped showing up by week 2. good at teaching though.
Worst lecturer I have had in my life. Just watch Chenflix or attend a different lecture
Can't teach, Exams are ridiculously hard

In addition to the course being challenging, he tends to over-explain things that really have no actual use to the students. For example, on the first content lecture of class, he took 10 minutes and drew two graphs to explain the rather simple Vector Distance Formula. Do the homework before class since it gives better explanations than him.

The lectures arevery difficult to follow as Yeung tends to go through material very quickly and has very sloppy handwriting. The grading is very exam heavy and the HW workload makes it difficult to find time to study for exams. He expects students to have a decent amount of calc experience that most did not have so you are playing catch up all year

Very good person but not as good of a lecturer only because symbols used through out lecture is very confusing and misleading, sometimes he'll make mistakes in his work and gets very confusing in the end. The material is simple but the vocabulary he uses to explain makes it difficult.

Exclusively speaks in esoteric jargon no one uses. Uses variables that look the same for different things in the same new equation he is writing poorly. Gives poor examples and oversimplifies them. Expects a degree of proficiency he does not exhibit (FREQUENTLY computation mistakes in lec). Watched a kid call his dad crying after a lec in week 4.

Always running out of time during the lecture, even though he teaches VERY fast, which makes me think either the whole curriculum has a problem or the professor needs an improvement on his teaching skills. He tends to mumble on the lecture, and it's frustrating. Also wasting time on keep mentioning "you should have learned this from high school".

The guy was awful at teaching and he couldn't stay focused on the lecture. One time, he wasted 15 minutes of lecture trying to figure out how to use the smart switch in the lecture hall to dim the lights since he thought that the projector was too dim in a fully lit room. People were literally yelling, saying they can see the projector just fine.
Can't understand him or his handwriting.... I learn better by just not going to class.

Great prof but the course it self is very difficult and we go through the material generally fast. He knows what he is talking about though and makes sure to give you enough examples to get through the homework. Exams are difficult, like all calc exams at Purdue.

He is boring for mot of the time, lectures fast paced. However once you get familiar, he is quite humorous at times. Hard grade and very few explanations, so you really need to either follow the lectures very tight or do some individual study on points unfamiliar.

Had Dr. Yeung as an instructor for Differential Equations last year. His lectures are way too fast-paced for me to sufficiently learn the material from going to class. One of my friends would often skip class, assuming to learn the material through Khan Academy. I think I understand why she did so much better in the class than me.

Not the best math professor I've ever had. He is hard to understand at times, often makes mistakes, and handwriting could be neater. That being said, he knows what he's talking about and his lectures can be helpful. Go to his office hours and read the textbook before/after his lectures as a supplement and you should be fine.

In the beginning of the semester he tells his students that he recommends and expects them to review the materials beforehand. This is probably the reason his lecture feels a bit fast-paced, but it is understandable as he is trying to cover as many example questions as possible. He is also helpful when asked for help in office hours.
Unlike other professors, Dr. Yeung goes over the material very fast. If you are good at math you will love his class. Because it always end up early.

Not a bad teacher I thought, but I did have an advantage as I had taken Calc in high school. I got a B for bombing 1 test. At times he was hard to understand because of his accent, and his handwriting, and he likes to work quickly. I could follow along for the most part though. And after every problem he would stop and ask if anyone had questions.
Avoid him. His lecture is not helpful to any tests and he always makes mistakes in lecture. Handwriting is difficult to read and sometimes does't know how to use the projector.

I was worried about this class given past negative reviews but ended up being pleasantly surprised. Prof Comer is always kind and an interesting lecturer. She goes out of her way to point out common misunderstandings and constantly connects material back to engineering applications. Be warned though, the grade was entirely exams.

Treat her class like a test-out exam; study everything independently. Attending lecture is a waste, she fails to impart any insight from the material. Exams are graded arbitrarily, you have to do things "her way", so be overly verbose wherever possible. In Fall 2025, the final exam (worth 40% of the class grade) was never even returned to us.
The only good thing about her is that the lectures aren't mandatory, and don't bother looking at the lecture notes she posts because her handwriting is illegible.

Copies notes and doesnt teach. Can't read a thing she writes. Can't solve problems and takes off points on exams for minor things like she doesnt understand the material. Apparently doesnt give Fs though to compensate. Take this course with someone else if you want to learn.
Lectures were slow and anything written down was illegible. Stopped going to lectures after a few weeks and learned better from online sources. Methods of teaching are simply esoteric.
bad notes, bad lectures, tough grader. Cannot  read handwriting. Just bad.

Awful professor, can't teach for sure. The class is extremely test heavy and averages are around 30-40%. That says a thing or two about Mary. She doesn't care about students or them learning. Class is a math class disguised as ECE. There is nothing electrical or computer going on in class. Its a weed out course to elect juniors.
Not bad, not good, not much of a curve. Exam 1 and 2 are not too bad so make sure you get as many points as you can on those because the final is rough.

There is no textbook, but she recommends following an old 2002 textbook as if probability and statistics has not advanced in 20 years. As such, her approach to even simple things such as pdf and cdf is archaic. Homeworks are not graded which makes sense because she does not provide ANY useful examples to even possibly help with them. AVOID ECE 600.

Professor Comer was a nice person, but she wasn't a great lecturer. Her lectures put me to sleep, and when I was awake enough to listen to what was going on, I could never follow along. Part of this was because of the material itself, but she definitely didn't help. She posts her lecture notes but her handwriting was very hard to understand.

hmm after courses go online, the length of the course videos shortened to around 5 minutes for some reasons I still don't understand. Earlier this semester before the pandemic, I was lucky enough to go to her lecture, which no one goes after the first several weeks, since she just copied(read) her notes verbatim, including her course website URL.
After the grades come out, you can not reach her any more. And she even does not release the final grades on Grade scope....... Speechless.

I usually don't write a review, but for her, I felt like I had to. Easily the worst professor I have ever had. Her lectures are basically her reading notes from her laptop while re-writing them on a piece of paper. Learned nothing from going to class. If you can, postpone this class for a later semester, if she is your only option.
I dont know why I took this.... if you dont have a textbook and previous understanding of probability.. you will die :)

Prof. Comer's lectures aren't intuitive to understand. She could focus on imparting conceptual insights via pictorial depictions and write down 'Key points' as class notes rather than writing down every single word told verbally. She could maybe provide her old notes that she's been using every year before class than writing the same once again.

If you are planning to take 600 under her, then you are probably not going to learn much. She doesn't teach concepts in class, just jots down notes that were perhaps prepared some years ago. Doesn't give intuition, doesn't use venn diagrams/figures to explain concepts. Regret paying my tuition fee for this course.

She is knowledgeable but I felt that her teaching methods were slightly monotonous and boring. Moreover, there is a lot of homework, but those account for only a very small percentage of your total grade. For the tests, you might end up getting a very low score if you don't really write answers exactly as she expects.

Her lecture is freakin boring. People dont show up to class. A study session by yourself is necessary. She wrote everything she told in lecture. She rarely gives partial point on partial credit sections.

Terrible professor. She doesn't use lecture slides and instead opts to hand-write barely legible notes. She doesn't teach out of the book so these notes are the only thing to study. Exams are extremely difficult and very easy to lose points quickly. This is the first course I'll have to retake, so if you have the option take it with a diff prof.

She just copy the notes in class and upload all the lecture notes and homework solutions once in a while. Office hours is useless. Exams are hard. She speaks too soft and her notes are pretty useless.
It feels like she doesn't care about teaching at all. No effort put into planing.

I think she is a good person and she does try to help if you go for her office hours. But she is not a great teacher. She just comes to class and copies notes. I don't even find her method of delivering lectures clear because she cannot explain things. She doesn't know how to solve some of the homework problems.
She understands her stuff well, but didn't put much effort in teaching. She was copying the notes from her prepared ones to lecture us, not too much on understanding or deduction.

A very difficult professor; do not be fooled. However, she will never make you feel as though your questions are trivial and will do her best to answer them. Hard to get outside help aside from her strict office hours, though. Overall: hard, but fair.
awful, evil, devil, the worst smelly teacher ever.

Wow I wish I had taken this class my first semester. As a freshmen you sometimes need a professor that cares and teaches well. The class was awesome. Plants are more amazing than I thought. You will love the course
It was a good class, if I had worked a little harder and took advantage of the in class extra credit point, I would have had an A. Great professor and she really cares about students

The class was challenging but I learned a lot of about plants, more than I thought I would. She is a good professor, smart, vibrant and does not play when it comes to learning. You will work hard for the grade but it's fair.

Exams are poorly made and she disrupts students during the exam by yelling at people for leaving too early. She talks down on students a lot. There are about 70 HW questions each week. When it comes to the semester project, that starts at the end of the semester, she is very rude about it and won't tell you group numbers or anything.

Poor lectures, tests are full of typos and incredibly specific, she will belittle the class for not answering in a large lecture hall, often ranting about how students do not study enough. She makes students use AI for a large final project, and has told us she wishes to use AI for grading (though I'm unaware if she actually has). Avoid this course
Easy content but there is a lot of it. She puts little to no effort into the exams, they are practically illegible, not a single question without typos.
Her teaching style was hard to follow

She is a very lovely lady but the work load is a lot. She isn't always the easiest to understand in lectures or recitation. I wasn't pleased when she didn't come to the final. If you take this course UTILIZE OFFICE HOURS. She helpful on a one on one setting.

Oh boy... She goes very quickly, hardly posts the slides, so many severe grammatical errors on quizzes, exams, and even the final. She didn't even show up for the final. Assigned a pointless group project with no instructions about artificial intelligence (...botany class?). Seems like a nice person, but no. HW will take about 3 hours per week.

Goes really fast through lectures slides, half the time doesn't post them. She didn't even show up for the final or send an announcement for us that week. Clearly knowledgeable on the topic and seems like a pleasant person, but her teaching style is very disorganized. Pop quizzes.. and recitation is mandatory.

I SWEAR ON MY ENTIRE LIFE THAT SHE CANNOT SPELL AT ALL. I asked a TA for help and he said that he also didn't know what she wanted and to "use AI and pray". Please find a professor that respects and values students and their time. She doesn't respond to emails and the multiple tedious weekly assignments make up a VERY small percentage of your grade
Dr. Fields isn't a very good lecturer and moves too quick. The exams also have very poor grammar.

This class was extremely challenging. The tests were confusing, and many questions weren't from lectures or labs. Lectures are very content heavy. I would not recommend taking a class with her. Find a prof who respects their students.

She is great. Fun, knowledgeable and gives good examples. She doesn't just stand up front and talk but interacts with us and that made the class more interesting. 

I don't even like science but she made it cool to learn about plants
just dont do it its not worth your time. The labs were the only thing I enjoyed because of the kind TA's
You will learn a lot but you will work for it. I learned more about plants and understand why they are so important now. She is a really nice professor.

Dr. Fields was great to have as a freshman. She helped us stay organized, and showed up to teach with a smile every day. She has a lot of energy in the morning... definitely made us wake up. We review in class often to break up the lecture, which is nice. She is knowledgeable and has good stories to tell about plants. i learned a lot in the class
She is smart, challenging, caring and cool.

This class focuses heavily on bad lecture slides which you NEED to memorize to do well on the weekly quizzes and tests. Both quizzes and tests are filled with errors. 

Recitations are usually canceled and the quizzes are 1-3 points.

Labs are a joke and take no more than an hour. 

If you did AP Bio the textbook is optional and you will get an A.

Professor often doesn't email students back when needed. Her exams cover nothing that was learned in class, very fast paced doesn't take the time to really explain the content. Recitation was cancelled more than it happened. Overall does not take the time to really focus on helping the students learn the material.

Class is good but fast paced, Dr. Fields is great att explaining difficult concepts and making class enjoyable.
You will learn a lot about plants and recitation group helped me learn a lot and find good study partners

Dr. Fields is one of the best professors I have had at Purdue. The class is organized and she provides weekly notes to follow everything. She is caring, knowledgeable and full of energy. Definitely wakes you up
For a morning class. The labs are cool, sometimes out of sink and the TA was not very excited to be in lab it seemed. I learned a lot.

Very confusing syllabus, not quite accurate but didn't update it - some assignments were never due.
Group project was a joke and literally just a waste of time. So was every lab (once a week).
Contradicted herself often.
No content was aligned. Exams weren't on same thing we learned in lecture, or recitation, or labs.
Overall learned very little

She's caring who is willing to meet her students where they are. She is knowledgeable, proficient, and passionate about the material. Personally, I loved taking her class but I know many people who did not feel the same way. Her communication and BrightSpace skills need help. Be ready to think through material; rote memorization won't get an A.

I've had Mrs.Gabrielov for two semesters now (RUSS 201-202).  If your are passionate and willing to learn, she will help you accomplish great feats.  She is the most caring and knowledgable professor in the Russian dept.  It was understandable but saddening when I learned of her retirement. Wishing all the best to her

Had Amina for multiple semesters and multiple classes in the RUSS curriculum. No sweeter or more lenient and kind human being. Genuinely wants you to learn content and seek learning outside of reading/homework. Incredibly forgiving and adapts to your level.

Amina is the single most kind and helpful professor I've had at Purdue. She answers all questions carefully, takes time to help people who are confused, and genuinely seems to care about every student. There are no meaningful flaws in her teaching, and her classes usually closely follow the textbook.

I took Gabrielova's Russian Fairy Tales class to fulfill an English requirement and I remain thankful to this day that I took her class. As someone who wasn't really interested in fairy tales to begin with, she certainly made me interested. It was an easy class and very straightforward. You cannot go wrong with this class as it is very interesting.

Amina was one of the kindest and helpful teachers I had at Purdue. She made me love Russian literature and motivated me to read more on my own, while she taught the significance of literature in Russian culture. You could tell she truly enjoyed her work and was very easy to talk to about any struggles you were having in or outside the class.

She is one of the best professors I have had while at Purdue. She puts an emphasis on learning Russian, while not making students grind to get an A. If I was not graduating, I would have taken Russian courses to 402. I will miss this class the most from Purdue
