She is the sweetest teacher I have had at Purdue. She is so passionate about helping students learn and applying cultural knowledge to the language.
She is the best
More than anything she just wants you to learn russian, I've taken tons of language classes and have absorbed an unbelievable amount of material from just a year working with her.  Great professor.
she's the best
Prof. Gabrielov is a great professor. She teaches very clearly and is relatively lenient. I would definitely recommend this class if you are interested in taking Russian.
Lenient on missed homework. She genuinely just wants you to learn Russian so she's pretty easy on missed assignments. I literally missed an exam and I got a 96 in the class as my final grade.
Prof. Gabrielova is great, very helpful and very patient when you don't understand something. Would recommend!
Great professor!
She is highly educated, extremely helpful, and genuinely cares about her students.  I loved attending her class!
Pretty much delegated everything to the TA, who had absolutely no empathy for working students. Save yourself some money and grief and take a different class!
Does not understand the needs of students working full time jobs. Save yourself some grief and don't take any classes with this professor.
Great Professor.
Has a huge research experience.
Very knowledgeable
ok course
Not useful..
Superficial knowledge
Really knowledgeable
Very Nice class

He seemed to be harsh during lectures, and was intolerant for any "weird behavior" during classes. Yet, he himself, as well as his phd students, helped a lot after class. Every time there were troubles on project and homework, he and his phd students would happily explain in detail. Of course, you have to at least give it a try before asking.

He doesn't care what you choose to study. He is very practical knowledge oriented and doesn't give too much attention to course or curriculum. So if you want someone like a tutor he isnt it. But, he does know his subject and if you want to do research, it'll be a great world of opportunities to be able to work with him.
He never prepared before class.

Very knowledgeable, but you can tell teaching isn't his priority. Class structure is very disorganized, nothing (exams, homework, presentations) is scheduled more than a week in advance. Uses old powerpoints that he hasn't updated or probably even looked at for several years.

He was out of town a lot for conferences and other meetings, as a result a sizeable portion of the class was taught by one of his graduate students during the semester. Not able to give his students a lot of time outside the class. He did not appear interested with the class and many a times rushed through many of the materials.
No Comments

Professor Mao teaches the material well enough in class that I never felt concerned about exams. Course is set up for students to succeed. Slides can be slightly confusing, and homework questions were sometimes tough, but recitation cleared up most issues. Clearly wants all students to succeed.
Really wants students  input on exams and how to better the course. TA's are very helpful and help a lot . Lecture attendance is encouraged. Gave a lot of example problems which helped a lot.

Lecture is generally slow with lots of examples. May be hard to understand at first because he is soft spoken, but once get used to it, the logic was clear. I find the lecture very helpful, it just requires a little patience at first. He is also very patient at answering questions on the spot.

Lectures are generally pretty poor, mostly example problems worked through far too slowly. Would frequently cover several important things in one day and spend far too long on unimportant details. Homework was a poor reflection of the lecture material. Exams were made by TAs and were okay. The only reason I learned anything in this class was SI.
Great professor, gave extra credit and was always very helpful.

Did not understand one thing that he said all semester. He is very difficult to listen to and once you catch on, he has moved on. Lecture slides do not help with exams. If you ignore his lectures and watch organic them tutor on YouTube, you will be fine.

He is actually a very good professor. I think he is definitely more soft-spoken, but it just made everyone move closer to the board, which in reality, is not all that bad. There are also only three grades which is different from most chemistry courses seen in prior years which can be stressful, but is definitely more manageable than 115/6.
He is hard to understand because he talks quietly and doesn't articulate well. The lecture slides are not very helpful. He often edits his slides during class to fix mistakes.
This class was awful, bad lectures, no notes, book is unhelpful, and there are only three grades in the whole class.
what a combo: the least useful class ever at Purdue and the most confusing instructors!!

ENGR 132 is a confusing class and the assignments can be tedious. However, Professor Aguilar was wonderful and made the 7:30am start time a little better. She was always willing to answer questions and responded to emails promptly. Her slideshows clearly conveyed the day's information.
She is awesome. Super super helpful and is willing to listen and check over work if you feel it the grading was off. Very kind and sweet to all students.

Amazingly chill professor. Prof Aguilar is very responsive with emails. She agreed to regrade an assignment in which I accidentally turned in a blank template and give me extra EYM points to an EYM I attended (wrong group) for me to get an A. In general very nice professor and just put some effort in for the assignments.
Her lectures were about 2 minutes long. Most of my class did bad on the exams, while other professors gave in depth lectures over the MatLab content. I found the exams to be difficult due to this.

She's super sweet and understanding, and clearly loves what she does. There was a snowstorm that prevented me from getting to campus safely, and she understood and allowed me to virtually do my work that week. The only thing is she's a little hard to understand at times due to her accent, but otherwise an awesome professor.

One of my favorite professors due to her high energy and insightful advice during class. Is always willing and able to help in class, but is difficult to reach during office hours and outside of class. Would highly recommend as a professor. Regarding the class itself, always check the grading criteria, be available to your teammates. Divide work.

Very clear about what she wants for assignments and projects, which made the class feel organized and manageable. Always quick to help over email and incredibly supportive, even with such a big class. Genuinely cares about her students. Fantastic professor.

ENGR 131 is a tedious class. The rubrics are vague, group work means that your grade depends on others, and if you don't pay attention at the beginning of the semester then the exams will be difficult as they are all Excel based. Prof. Aguilar is cheerful, caring, and can answer any question. I would recommend taking her class if at all possible.

Professor Aguilar was the perfect professor for this class. Concise but effective lecturing, with more than enough time to work in class. She is kind and caring, and answers any questions you have. The grading is generally quite fair, and when you feel that it's not, the TA's in the class are very accessible to help fix your grade.

Prof. Aguilar is one of the best ENGR131 instructors you can have. She cares about all of her students and genuinely wants them to succeed. She is available if you ever have a question and responds to emails extremely quickly. Make sure to cross your t's and dot your i's because the grading is extremely harsh for small mistakes.

The class was quite easy. There was one period with a heavy workload with a project due and a test in the same week, but this was an exception. She gives extra credit at the end and is willing to fix grades if you believe the grade you received is unfair. She is also always willing to answer questions and provide help on confusing assignments.

She is super charismatic, always cares about her students, replies to emails very quickly, and is very understanding. If you have any problem, just talk to her and she will give you extra time to complete your assignments. She is an excellent professor

Aguilar is probably one of the best professors there is for 132. She's kind, caring, and explains things clearly. Our section's GTA was the complete opposite (rude, uncooperative) however, if you don't agree with a GTA's take on your grade for an assignment, you can always go to Aguilar to get it fixed.

She is super sweet and always clear at the beginning of class what she expects on the assignments, when the deadlines are, how to work the MATLAB code etc. My team had major issues personally, and she was super helpful to working with us and was lenient. She's probably one of the best profs you can have for a class structured like ENGR132

At the start, I had trouble with keeping up with the class, not understanding everything that was being said. Over time, you get used to proficiency, but I felt like the studio/lecture part was rushed and that more productivity could be have done in lecture rather than free time. Also, I would mostly read class slides rather than listen.
A kind and understanding professor. Not difficult to understand, made grade expectations clear. Was flexible with missed assignments and grade curving at the end of the semester.

Despite the course being pathetically structured (not the professor's fault), Professor Aguilar did the best she could to make it somewhat educational and enjoyable. She was always ready to help with any problems we had with assignments and would go beyond what any other professor would do.

Prof. Aguilar was a very nice professor who took the time to help students when facing a problem. A lot of the course relies on group projects, but communication with Prof Aguilar and the GTA can improve your situation if you have a bad group. The work done in class prepares you to do well on the exams and in later-level engineering classes.
Flew through the slides and didn't explain much. Easy exams and work though.

Hello freshman, here's what you need to know about Aguilar's ENGR131.

1. This class is a points game. Always read the rubrics (course tools>assignments). Half the class is between 93-95%, and 94% is an A. Get every point you can.

2. Get a good group. You may be unfortunate and get people that decide they can get away with not doing anything.

Totally recommend this class! My team and I learned so much, and going with Prof. Aguilar is the way to go. She's always there to help, gives awesome programming tips, offers solutions to problems, and really listens to our CATME feedback. Plus, she's super cool to work with.

Awful class in general. The grading is all over the place, and almost none of the concepts you need for assignments would actually be taught during class. The prof is okay, but she can be difficult to understand at times due to her accent and pace. The groups are a hit or miss, but group projects weigh heavily in this class, so hope for the best.

Professor Aguilar will always take the time to help her students truly understand the material. She does lots of examples that are very relevant to the assignments. She is so kind and makes an effort to get to know her students, which I really appreciate.

I love Professor Aguilar! Coding can be difficult if you are new. PLEASE reach out to her if you need help. She always willing to help you with HW and other parts of the class. She is so nice and caring. Do your work and try in the class and you will be fine. The group work 2nd semester is annoying but doable if you have a good group.

Pr. Aguilar is my sun on a gloomy day. Makes your day better. The class is very tough if you are not a coding pro, but she makes is as easy as possible. If you get a bad project partner, TALK TO HER! Do not hesitate! She will do whatever she can to help you. If you feel sick, tell her: she'll let you stay home. You missed assignment?  get extension

Professor is really passionate about this course, he made lectures easy to understand and he was not a strct grader and wanted the students to learn rather than be picky on the homework assignments. FInal project was open ended and easy as well would take again

He is incredibly difficult to understand. He was a pain to deal with for DRC accommodations - to the point where the director needed to be involved - and he still was not cooperative. His lack of interest in creating an equitable learning environment is unacceptable. The TAs for the class are great if you need help.

Expect to teach yourself everything. The exams are a guessing game for which problems he is going to pull from the textbook. There is little help or guidance within the class, or any structure at all. If you are not required to take this class, I would stay far away.

100% the worst professor I've had at this school. Mumbles during lectures, goes off on tangents, and admittedly said he shouldn't be teaching this class. Gives almost no resources, and what is given is not helpful at all. Attendance is mandatory but you're more likely to get confused listening to him than learning online. Avoid this professor.

Refuses to follow the syllabus and teach the entire chapter, he will pick and choose his favorite parts of the chapter and teach them, while he expects you to learn everything on your own. He will turn you down during his office hours. In lecture, he won't take questions, or will just be very condescending while responding.

This class as stated by Prof. Choi is taught by the Nuclear Department so they can meet credit hours taught for their department. Its very clear how little care goes into this class both from the department and Prof. Choi. In NUCL 273 you are given nothing and expected to teach yourself because Prof. Choi does not posses that ability.

He is very difficult to understand, talks very quiet and the microphone doesn't help. When he was told about some students not being able to hear him, he just switched the seating arrangement, so the ones who couldn't hear could and the ones who could hear couldn't. He occasionally will get sidetracked.

Went to his office hours on Mon and Wed for two weeks in a row and he rejected me every time because he had too many papers to grade. He is unprofessional and unorganized. I don't understand how he expects students to pass if he doesn't give us the time of day.

He is a traditional professor, but one of the BEST professors I have ever had. A lot of the class falls on you, but he is very knowledgeable and understands the material. Though he taught this class for the first time last semester, he was a perfect fit. I learned so much from him and from the class. Go to his office hours!!

Took 460 and 560 with Professor Choi, and would love to take any other class he offers. Gave great insight into the fusion industry, and always welcomed questions. While his classes are a little difficult, he always made sure we were understanding and gave extra references to outside resources.

I get why everyone is upset over Professor Choi, but he really tries to make sure everyone passes the course. For me, it's not about how you begin; it's about how you finish. The first exam was definitely tough, but Professor Choi made improvements for the second exam and final by providing a guide that outlined the questions that would appear.

Do not be scared off by bad reviews, Professor Choi is a fine teacher. He will do his best to alleviate any concerns you have if you go and meet with him. Come to the class comfortable with prerequisite content and study outside of lectures and you will understand everything perfectly.

This is the worst course I have taken in my life. Useless lecturers filled with tangents, a 27% exam average which he blames on our inability to be engineers, and incredibly rude in class and office hours. Exams are very easy but the grading is nonsense and no one understands course content. Just memorize the question banks and you'll pass.

I am not a fan of rating professors, but I felt the need for this professor. This professor doesn't provide notes to you and yaps for most of the class. His lectures are mostly of him reading off the slide and sharing answer keys of the textbook questions without explanation.

People were not kidding about him, he seems really nice but this is probably one of the worst courses I've taken at Purdue. He just talks all class about random things and expects you to understand the material. 40 minutes of yapping and 10 minutes of going through the formulas we need for the HW.

Covered way too much material for an introductory class and was not helpful for exam prep at all. He is fairly lenient with the grading. He provides no homework solutions so be prepared to figure everything out on your own. Buy the textbook.

He made the class very hard, covering material that did not need to be covered in an introductory course. Lectures are somewhat hard to understand, but attendance is required. Studying for exams is stressful as he does not make it clear what formulas will be given. However, he is generous when giving grades, and does not want to see people fail.

This professor is probably the worst professor that I have had in my four years at Purdue. His lectures are wful, he just reads off of a pdf of the notes that he provides you. Then come exam time there is no standard format to help you study. He just tells you know everything that you've covered.

Professor Choi is very kind and is more than willing to help outside of class, however his lectures are hard to follow. He doesn't follow the textbook, but assigns homework from the textbook making it hard to complete. This might be for the better though because the textbook is terrible. Homework takes a long time to complete. Exams are difficult.
Interesting but very difficult class.

I had professor Choi in my Fusion Space Propulsion elective class for engineering. He is incredibly enthusiastic about teaching the subject but he very quickly turns a 1 credit hour course into a 3-4 credit hour course. Expect to be put into groups and to work much more than you would expect outside of class to get things done.

Dr. Choi is a great professor, but students need to be engaged. If you care and try, he will bend over backwards for you. He's willing to do whatever you need for you to succeed, but communication is key. Most exam material is almost directly from the notes. Don't miss class, and take good notes.

Prof. Choi is a great teacher. He really wants to help students understand the material, and he never minds going back, rephrasing things that don't make sense, etc. He is a great guy and one of the best teachers I've ever had!
Worst professor I have ever had. Grade was completely arbitrary, and he never gave one assignment back so nobody knew how they were doing.

Prof. Choi is great! He really cares about the students learning and goes into useful tangents about scientific history and principles. Has unique descriptions for things that serve as great mnemonics.

Grading is slow and very harsh. We had work from Jan/Feb not graded until May! Attendance was technically not mandatory but in-class group assignments every class says otherwise. Dr. Temi was also not understanding with special situations surrounding grades/attendance and several of us *almost* missed GPA minimums for awards due to this class only.

Dr. Temi grades slowly and harsh. Assignments from early Jan/Feb not being graded until May! The entire course is a group project, if absent you won't get credit for the day's assignment. Dr. Temi also was not flexible with special circumstances regarding attendance or grades, and definitely hit quite a few people's GPAs. I wouldn't take her again.

Dr. Temi's grading is SLOW and STRICT. Huge assignments from early Jan/Feb being graded in May! Course is all group work, if absent you get no credit for the day's assignment. Really hard to plan for future grades needed or missing classes because nothing is updated. She also does not care about special circumstances regarding absences or grades.
Do not be scared Dr. Temi is super caring and the course is light work. Literally no homework whatsoever and it's a free A. Maybe my standards are low but she was nice and the course was easy.

I've never seen such a disorganized professor in my entire life. You will be snowed under with unnecessary and unclear work and spend hours on daily basis for assignments that seem like they are made up just to fill the syllabus. She is extremely tough grader and her feedback is highly ambiguous.

All of the concerns raised in previous reviews are accurate and align with my experience in this course. The class felt consistently disorganized, with unclear structure and expectations throughout the semester. Instructions for assignments were often ambiguous, which made it difficult to understand what was required. Stay away from Dr. Temi.

Professor Olenloa is extremely disorganized and consistently fails to provide clear or coherent instructions for assignments, making expectations difficult to understand. Feedback is vague and unhelpful, offering little to no guidance on what was done correctly or how to improve, which significantly undermines the learning experience.

Overall, not the worst Honors First Year Experience Professor, she was just disorganized and the rubrics were a bit vague. Not hard, but would advise choosing a different professor. She teaches Student Motivation, which may be helpful to some but wasn't to me personally. She was really kind, however, and was passionate about her area of expertise.

Dr. Temi's class tends to be disorganized and often confusing. Content can be hard to find, expectations are unclear. She messed up grades for multiple students in my class. She grades very easily on the assignments only to be harsh on the final. Grades sometimes don't make sense. I felt like I left the class frustrated and learning little.

Dr. Temitope is one of the most disorganized professors I have ever seen. While she has a generally good attitude, she is extremely unclear with instructions, directions, and assignments. Stuff is posted at the last minute or not at all, with very little clarification. Ultimately, the class feels very much like a waste of time and energy. Avoid her

While criticisms of the class are justified (see other reviews here, they are mostly correct), this is rate my *professor*, not rate my class. I am still considering the class in my rating, but Dr. Temi herself was extremely generous and helpful when I had questions or concerns on non-class issues, and clearly cared deeply about student well-being.
She was insufferable. She treated the class like preschoolers. Just stay away from Temitope.
Class was literally pointless and no work, just pissed me off being in her presence.

I don't think I've ever taken a class as disorganized and mismanaged as this class. Dr. Temi seems very lost on what the expectations and ultimate goals of the peer mentor program are. She also insists we do "roleplays" for the class which, frankly, are nothing if not a pointless exercise with poor grading criteria.

I am currently 2 weeks into this 4 week course about mentoring given over the summer and so far I can confidently say that this teacher, along with the class has been nothing short of catastrophically and appallingly abysmal.

Professor Zhang does an excellent job at making complicated concepts simple and looping back on previous content to ensure you remember the material. Complex analysis was extremely interesting, and I also love that he gave proofs when possible. The class is overall very good as long as you attend lectures and actually learn the content.

Professor Xiangxiong is excellent and very organized. He explains concepts clearly, repeats key ideas, and summarizes them well. He is very responsive on Piazza and always willing to help. I really enjoyed the course and would highly recommend him to future students.

(MA 574 is a grad topics class) Dr. Zhang's lectures are well paced and informative, spending extra time on difficult material. Passing 574 is not very hard, but concepts are difficult and take time & effort to fully grasp. Notes for all of his classes are available on his website, and he's very quick to respond to emails. Flexible office hours.
He knows what he is talking about.

Overall not bad. The course material was pretty easy, but he did go over a lot of topics that were very briefly discussed and not mentioned on homeworks or exams, and then proceeded to load them onto the final. However, he answers your questions quickly online and is pretty lenient on grading.

Weird teaching style and arrangement. For elementary linear algebra he taught a lot of concepts which are not in the course syllabus, and introduced inadequate basic content. Though it is useful academically but unfortunately too abstract and frustrating for beginners in linear algebra. He is also not patient enough in the office hours.
This is the kind of professor that this website was made to warn about.

Goes through concepts many times in homework and class and makes sure that the students understand well. Notes are kinda difficult to read sometimes but are still very useful for reviewing concepts. Homework is very relevant to classes and helps you further understand the topics. Final grade was 80% exams (2mids+1final) but we were well-prepared
Responsible, approachable, intelligent
The grading on homework is tough and strict. Things learnt in this class is hard, but the office hour can be helpful. Except for the hard staff learnt, his explanation is clear enough.

Home work and text book is so useful that you can review what he have though in class a good way to review for the exam. Even though the office hour held only once per week, but you can always welcome to make an extra appointment with him
He tries, but he doesn't explain things too well. If you are to take his class, using a lot of imagination might make things a bit better. Also, be prepared for very harsh grading.
Prof. Zhang is really good at offering different perspectives rather than directly reading from the text. To me,  the lecture notes are very useful.
Good

I did well in the class overall, but the exams were graded very strictly. In some cases, points were deducted even when the answer was correct if the method didn't match what she wanted. Also, she is hard to understand. I do not recommend taking a class with her
Difficult to understand, harsh grader, lots of grading mistakes,

Had this prof for mgmt 388, and unfortunately, was the only option for 544 this semester. Not engaging, difficult to understand, unclear group projects, and all of this resulted in a below 50% second midterm average. If the average is failing in a 500 level class, something is wrong
aced everything and still hate her
Incredibly difficult to understand and grades unfairly. Uncooperative with students and refuses to help push students in the right direction.
professor is not good at explaining things in the lecture and isnt very engaging

Project instructions were consistently vague, and the final project was graded unfairly. Feedback was returned too late to incorporate, yet most of the class received unnecessarily low grades. Points were deducted for not being “creative” despite using queries taught in class. TA guidance also conflicted with grading.

Poorly organized, hard to follow. She had 1 assginment in Excel and 2 in Tableau. The course is a TABLEAU class, there shouldn't be Excel assignments! Spacing between due dates was bad. GRILLED project presentation groups in front of all students. Her grading was inconsistent, there was hidden grading criteria which many people got points off for.
Doesn't know how to speak properly, cannot understand
Hard to follow during lectures. Class was poorly structured.
She does not know how to teach, consistent issues and wrong queries/data taught.
He's an excellent professor...go to his lecturs go to his reviews...only got good scores on the exams cause of him
It's obvious Lukens cares about his students. I didn't do well in the course but he got me through it.

Very good 20002 professor. Great lecturer: good pace, explains things clearly, posts notes. Replaced pop quizzes with pop attendance for his section, which given that exams are most of the grade was great, it reduced stress. Exams are reasonably difficult but quite fair. The homework takes some time but is somewhat useful in practicing problems.

The instructor was clear in presenting the material. Exams were difficult but fair, and all content was drawn from lecture and the textbook. While homework differed in style, the practice exams aligned well with the actual exams.
Lukens is an amazing professor who clearly knows the subject matter. He is very passionate about amplifiers. He is also really funny and makes silly jokes during lectures.

I felt that Prof Lukens did a poor job explaining content during lectures. His lectures were disorganized, and he often spent way too much time on theory instead of solving example problems. He also does not post lecture slides or recordings. I still attended his lecture, but I found more success studying the textbook and other online resources.

ECE2k2 is a very challenging class, as the grade is weighted heavily on difficult exams, however Lukens did a good job of teaching the class. He always answers questions during lectures and clearly cares about the students learning. I do wish that he would post his lecture notes on brightspace and be more active on the Piazza though.

Professor Lukens is an amazing 2K2 instructor (at varying points Makin, Kildishev, and Jung filled in for our lecture, so I can confidently say Lukens was the best). When he teaches he writes on a piece of paper, so it's very easy to follow along compared to using a slideshow. He also gives helpful tips on upcoming exams.

Lukens did a great job teaching 2k2 for his first time on this course, especially considering 2k2 to be one of the most obtuse and difficult classes at Purdue. Lukens is a caring professor, grades fairly, and works with each and every student to achieve success.

2k2 is not a fun course, but Lukens did an amazing job, especially for it being his first time teaching a college class. He always made sure to answer questions in class and genuinely seemed to care about students. OH were helpful. Taught directly from the book, but got all important material covered. Would recommend and I hope he keeps teaching!

Dr. Liu did a great job in explaining signal processing. The course itself is very complicated, but it can be handled with proper mathematical background and studying. The lectures are great, and the tests are generally difficult. The exams are a large percent of the grade, but they are all doable. He really takes time to cover the content deeply.

He's a great guy, but to be honest is a really bad professor. I had him for dynamics and he was not good at all at explaining things, to the point where I just went to a different professor's lecture. He is very hard to understand and not very good at explaining new material, tending instead to just read off the slides. Would not recommend.

Yangfan is a very nice and knowledge professor. However, His lecture note is not helpful for exam. I personally couldn't get the key points from his lecture, and did poorly for exams. If you a self-learner I would recommend him, but if your performance is heavily depend on the professor's explanation, then he is not your choice.

The ONLY criticism is that he can be hard to hear, unless you come to class early and sit in the front. If you can get past that, you have a gem of a teacher who will help you to the best of his abilities. He knows the material well, explains it well, and is always willing to take questions.
Professor Liu truly cares about his students and shared more about his personal life which made him more approachable. Sometimes hard to understand, but came to class prepared.
Professor Liu is a really nice instructor. He is very helpful during office hours. He is very supportive to students.
Nicest prof I've had in a long time. Sometimes hard to understand but he obviously cares for his students. Lectures are optional but going to them can be worth it to earn more points.
Solid thermo prof.

Devah's lectures are clear and concise, giving you enough detail to effectively learn the material covered in the homework and exams without getting bogged down in minutia. His office hours were also really helpful and rarely attended, giving plenty of one-on-one time to cover concepts I didn't get the first time around
Great professor.

Dr. Devah is one of the best lecturers I have had in ME. He clearly communicates concepts and his examples are useful. His slides are the right level of detailed, and he explains content very well. Attendance is based on 5 random quizzes, but he gives the class a heads up beforehand. His exam reviews are very useful, also.

Gibbs is the man. Actually knowledgeable about the subject matter and passionate to boot. Cares a lot about the students and their careers as a whole. There are daily quizzes, so make an effort to at the very least skim the material and make note of the terminology. The final exam is a cinch too; as long as you've tried on the quizzes you're good.

Genuinely one of the best professors I have had at DSB. He is genuinely a breathe of fresh air amongst the average professor. Attendance is mandatory but the lectures are intriguing and very helpful. He is always ready to help a student and is very flexible and responsive. 10/10 teacher fr.

Professor Gibbs is amazing. He genuinely made a very boring topic so much fun. Daily quizzes seem daunting at first but you are allowed to take them even if you miss a day and they are pretty straightforward. He adds memes to his slides, engages with his students and is overall an amazing professor. Thanks AG

Professor Gibbs is one of the best professors I've had at Purdue. He cares about the topics and his students. He's very accommodating/positive throughout class. There aren't exams throughout the term, just the final. The only downside is reading and taking a short quiz before every class. no surprises with this course, smooth sailing through.
He gave very vague answers to students questions. He kept teaching right up until the final. Also buried us with homework, including a final project due two days before the final.
He lets me get extensions Junior year. Nobody else likes that.

Dr. Tackett is one of the best ChE professors at Purdue. His lectures are very clear and taught extremely well. The homework can be difficult and time-consuming but he does a good job of helping students after class or in office hours. He is also overall a funny and very nice guy.

The professor is totally awful. I would suggest staying away from him. He simply creates vague pptx and does not understand anything technical. The final project was out in the blue without any proper guidance. i simply do not understand how and why he joined academia when he can't be technical on a single topic

Class is a glorified, extended powerpoint. Syllabus states 2 lectures per week, but after the first two weeks he decided to add a third 1 hour lecture per week. Zero direction on final project worth 40% of our grade, and is not even briefed or guided properly in any syllabus/documentation. Ask anyone in this class and all anyone could say is pptx

Attend the zoom meetings as they are awesome and its really only through interactions like those that you get to fully comprehend how intelligent and how much she wants you to not only succeed in class but also understand the underlying concepts.

I really appreciated Prof. Chaterji's contagious excitement in her lectures. The material she provided in video compared to reading material covered a wide variety and she introduced the class to some great online resources as well. Unfortunately, Chaterji does not have any more courses in the MSDS program at this time or I would take those too.
Terrific prof. Best prof in this series and I would recommend taking Prof. Chaterji's offering every Spring.

Applied Machine Learning by Dr. Chaterji is one of the most fulfilling courses you can take if you are in the MSAI/MSDS programs. She is extremely caring and genuinely wants to help her students succeed. You will code in Python and go over topics including regression, linear algebra, unsupervised/supervised learning, and neural networks. Take her!

Professor Chaterji was extremely knowledgeable and very good at conveying information in an interesting and compelling way. I am new to post graduate studies so I am unsure what the standard is, but I did not know any grades for the class until after the entire class had finished. Other than that I felt I learned a lot, she is a great professor.

- Dr.Chaterji and the TAs are very helpful and make sure the class is interactive and constructive. The course begins at an easy level but progresses to be very advanced. 
- The course materials are highly effective and engaging but tend to be elaborate at times.
- Overall this course is a good fit for beginners and rewarding for ML enthusiasts.

In fast-evolving technologies such as Machine Learning, Vision Systems, and AI, Professor Chaterji makes sure that all of the papers to read reflect the state-of-the-art or more recent advances. This class provides in-situ developments in these areas, providing first-hand insights into internationally recognized and cited papers.

Professor Chaterji is super enthusiastic and full of energy. The course is very good for someone who's getting started with the basics of machine learning. Got to read a lot of good papers and learnt a lot. The TAs are exceptional and they helped us throughout the semester.
The topics are both beneficial and engaging. Additionally, she brings a lot of energy and enthusiasm to the subjects she teaches.
She is really energetic and enthusiastic about the topic.

The professor did an excellent job supporting the lectures with lecture notes and relevant journal articles that support the trends in ML and CV!  Dr. Chaterji is also very knowledgeable about the field and current trends. The course took us from basic early ML concepts to advanced concepts used in recent years, which was very informative.
Terrific content and innovative lectures with top papers. Motivating!

The course covered broad areas including various deep learning approaches as well as model deployment in mobile/edge devices. Professor was there in the class all the time but TAs took turns to teach the class. The class emphasis was on latest state of the art in the field of computer vision.
A great combination of fundamental and advanced AI/ML concepts. Professor and TAs were caring and knowledgable.

The material was covered in an easy-to-understand manner. The course was a nice mix of foundational and advanced ML topics. I really liked the presentations where I got to dive in to one subject area and "teach" it to the class. Overall, it was a good experience!

Prof Chaterji is enthusiastic and absolutely delightful. I loved taking her class! There were a lot of papers, podcasts, articles, and homework that really helped me thoroughly understand the concepts. There were a lot of opportunities to revise and reinforce. I highly recommend this class for beginners and advanced ML students alike.

Dr. Chaterji is an exceptional professor who brings a contagious level of energy to each lecture. Her ability to cover a wide range of topics with depth and clarity is impressive. She creates an engaging learning environment and foster a passion for the subject in her students. Highly recommended.

Amazing blend of foundation computer science and machine learning and finishing with the latest in computer vision and computer systems. Lots of papers, including optional readings, and terrific podcasts and tech speakers. Loved it.

Overall, the course is designed to thoroughly review popular concepts and algorithms in broad Machine Learning and Deep Learning. The majority of the course is lecture-based, with a few paper readings and presentations. I was able to learn and grasp the concepts that I was interested in and also enjoyed participation through presentations.

The lectures were engaging and informative, and the assignments helped solidify my understanding of the material. Prof. Chaterji was knowledgeable and approachable, and the course was well-organized and structured. Overall, I found this course valuable, and I feel more confident applying machine learning and vision techniques to IoT projects.

The course comprised of the latest study material, was up-to-date with the ongoing research developments in the field, and was extremely well organized. It was interspersed with trivia that made learning fun and interactive. We learned not only the low level concepts, but also the high level applications and motivation.

Energetic and knowledgeable. This may be one of the best courses in ABE. Excellent preparation for seniors and grads who want an intuitive understanding of data engineering and machine learning concepts, taught in a very intelligent manner. The exams are also very helpful. I am a novice in machine learning and this prepared me well for my PhD.
There is a lot of research orientation to this teaching. Wish it was catered to my understanding and less about the prof's research.
She has lots of content that is beneficial for learners.

I took an online class. I especially appreciated the open and exploratory nature of the assignments and the final. The open endedness along with the hints makes you think of your own questions, and how would you apply it in a Real life scenario.

I  am a decent student and I am a good test taker. That said, this is the worst class I have ever taken at Purdue. She is rude, the environment is not welcoming to learn. She moves fast, her accent is difficult to understand, and her attendance policy is all or nothing. The class is stressful, and it was just not a beneficial learning experience.

This class was very difficult and felt defeating. I understood the homework but failed the exams. If you ask her a question, she often said, "Look at the book," when I already had and still didn't understand. The class may not have been so difficult if she had slowed down, answered questions, and provided more opportunities to earn points.

Professor is not nearly as bad as people say, she teaches pretty well and attendance policy is pretty calm (ask/answer x questions over the semester). I enjoy her classes and this attendance format and the exams are pretty reasonable / similar to homework and class examples. She does use a calculator when unnecessary though.
Took 413 with her. This was the worst class of my life.

I can't say I have enjoyed her class at all. The exam was incredibly hard compared to the practice problems and her ability to communicate is nonexistent. I wouldn't recommend in the slightest. Take class with someone else.

Please do not take this class if there are other options available. Grade is basically fully decided by final exam. She cannot solve and gets tripped up on her own in class practice questions. Expects you to be able to complete question perfectly after seeing one example that she struggles to solve. Struggles to answer in class questions as well.

Homework + Attendance = 30%, Midterm = 35%, Final Exam = 35%.  Extremely hard exams.  I felt like I could've just read the slides and gotten the same info instead of having to show up to class.  My raw grade for the overall class was a 53.33%, so the class curve was around 25%.  She can be funny sometimes, but overall usually boring.

If you have any other options, absolutely do not take her class. The practice problems she gives in class are almost unhelpful for the exams, and she can't even solve the problems she presents on her own slides. And you are not allowed to ask a question.

Not a bad person but her course is extremely rigorous. Make sure that you get a 70% overall on your iclicker grade to get a 100 in participation, if not you will receive a 0% even if you get a 68% on iclicker. The exams are hard. The 3.0 curve will round everyone up tremendously because of how low everyone's grade is.

Solely taught by reading off the slides, she seemed to believe that if she showed something/did a problem once, you immediately would understand the concepts and how to do them. Final grade determined by 3 exams and homework, leading to many students to drop after failing the first exam. If this class is required for you, good luck.

She's nice but the class is hard. I think most of us walking out of the second/final exam felt bad. I think the main issue is sometimes with how fast she goes. Be prepared to spend a lot of time studying if you have mgmt 310 with her.

Not good at all. She is super hard to understand and mumbles a lot. Her examples in class are unhelpful and she makes things more complicated and confuses more than helps. It's hard to get a good grade in this class since each exam is 35% and there are only 2 the whole semester so if you have a bad day then your whole grade is screwed.
She is well meaning but very difficult to work with if you have a question. She won't tell you the average score on the exam unless you go to class? Never seen that before...

Even if she were to slow down and speak clearly, her slides don't give clear direction or computations. She does not take the time to truly teach concepts. For our last exam, homework for chapters 12-14 were non-credit, as she didn't leave time to teach them in class, but they will be on the exam. You will be teaching yourself.

She obviously cares about students and seems like a nice person, but the lectures are very hard to follow with her accent and the material is quite boring. No point in going to lecture. 75% of your grade is going to be exams but if you read the textbook and practice they are fairly easy.
i wouldn't check the streets when crossing when exam season came around!

I would recommend another professor. She reads off of slides with an accent that is hard to understand. I can tell she cares for students but explains concepts too fast and can make things overly complicated. Exams are the majority of your grade and are pretty tough. iclicker quizzes are used every class. I have to teach myself everything.

If you can avoid her do so. her lectures are confusing and 70% of your grade is from two exam. the slides make no sense whatsoever and she teaches too fast in class for people to keep up. this is not clear enough for a prereq class

You cannot understand anything she is saying even with a microphone. She reads directly from her very unorganized slides, hardly teaching anything. Really wish I knew that I would be teaching myself the entire course material... there's a reason why no one shows up to class!! Exams at 25% each, setting you up for failure with no assignments.
She does not do a good job at explain and breaking down problems and concepts in the class. She does not do many example problems and rarely explains how to do a problem.

She's a good person, unfortunately her accent makes lectures difficult to understand. Slides are also more often confusing than not. Lectures are only somewhat related to topic, ended up just reading the textbook instead.

Moves way too fast and says to “understand, not memorize,” but doesn't make the material clear or digestible. Lectures are dry, slides are overloaded, and it's hard to know what matters. Test averages were much lower than other sections. I did better just reading the textbook than paying attention in class. Recommend another prof if possible.

310 with Yatsenko is easy if you pay attention and study. Her tests involve plugging numbers into formulas from your cheatsheet. She teaches well, providing examples and iClickers are like exam questions, so there are no surprises. Her reviews shouldn't be this bad.

Surprised by her low rating—likely because 310 is tough. Lots of content, hard exams, but practice materials help. She's knowledgeable, keeps class on track, and prepares you well, even if she's newer to teaching.

She cares a lot about her students and how much effort they put into the course. She has an accent that can be difficult to understand, but you get used to it after a few lectures. The exams are close to the practice exams, but are proven to be a bit tricky.

Look, he's not a bad guy, but his lectures can be really confusing even if you are paying attention. The lab is taught with many materials from the previous professor and if you get a bad TA for lab, you are on your own to figure it all out for the most part with the lab manual and a video. Quiz on each chapter of the book. Big group project too.
Good guy, but I don't appreciate him not being able to come to class on time. So far has been late to every lecture. If the professor is not expected to show up in time, neither should I.

Dr. Ledbetter goes on tangents that take away from his slides. The slides already skips lots of exam material. You'll end up reading the textbook anyways, so the slides feel a bit redundant (though they can help).

He cares about his students. Beyond that the lack of attention span makes the lectures highly uninformative, therefore the tests were very hard because we didn't learn 80% of what was on the tests. Read the book, don't listen to the lecture. Who knows where it will lead anyways.

He yaps about very random things during lecture (one time spent a class talking about an ostrich fable with an AI generated image of it on the screen). I didn't feel like I learned anything when he was talking. His slides that he goes through leave so much stuff out, but the textbook is helpful. Mostly I personally didn't like the material tho lol

He goes on soo many tangents about unrelated topics, the entire room couldn't care less to pay attention in his classes. He doesn't seem to actually prepare the lectures or he's just really bad at teaching, because he overcomplicates a 5 minute concept. Passive aggressive emails are a bonus. Bring earbuds to class.

(Cnit322) He shows up 10 minutes late to class and then expects people to show up and take attendance. He goes off topic very easily so you're gonna have to self-study. He plays AI-generated lectures and say he's “introducing new technology”. It feels like a waste of tuition and time, and Polytech shouldn't give him the next contract.

Seems like a nice guy but is not good at explaining topics at all. Gets side tracked very easily and skips many slides. You have to rely almost fully on the book for info. Lab difficulty depends on TA. Tests are all online and are not too bad. Big group project at the end, not very hard content but it is difficult to split up work as a group.

Very kind and evidently caring professor. Class can be a little disorganized/confusing at times, however, as you're basically on your own for the labs. TA's didn't cover or explain any of the material and released lab grades only just before lab exams so you had little chance to see anything of concern. Also, an A is a 94+ only so 93.9 is still A-.

This was the worst class I've ever taken in Purdue. If possible, please cancel the summer vacation courses. The experience was extremely poor. There was no effective assistance and no real-time courses available. Before I signed up, a friend said he was better suited for conducting research rather than teaching, I completely agreed with her.

The professor graded a month late, used another professor's recordings instead of teaching, and even reused last year's Lab 5 as this year's Lab 6 without renaming it. Group projects were harshly graded—teammates' mistakes hurt your grade, and explanations were dismissed. Late work got a zero.
Honestly this guy is a super nice individual. Goes on rambles sometimes but I never seemed to mind. Great person overall very nice and understanding.
Great guy. Lectures aren't the most productive, but 180 lab is much more important than lec. Weekly quizzes online. Honestly not the hardest class, use quizlet for your quiz and exam studying.

This instructor should refrain from teaching/offering this course in the summer. From ignored questions in emails, to practically your-on-your-own convoluted labs, to a situation where I submitted a lab "incorrectly" and it instantly earned me a F with no feedback left whatsoever, it seems those who are taught in person have it better.

Pretty chill class. Most people don't go to the lectures, but I found the content somewhat interesting. He is an okay lecturer, does go off on tangents at times but it's not like the material is super hard to understand so it's fine. Assignments and exams are pretty straightforward, with a group project at the end.
Very chill guy. Lectures can be boring but very nice and helpful. No complaints really.

The professor just read off the slides, you can get a good grade by just going through the information yourself. Labs were easy, I enjoyed them. There was a final group project, it was okay just get a good group. It is based on the labs and is pretty simple. Was relatively understanding when issues came up, especially with groups.

Very caring and engaging lecturer. Grading standards for the research paper draft that you submit every few weeks then piecemeal together into a research paper at the end of the semester are very inconsistent even after referring to the rubric. Exams on the other hand rely on rote memorization, but the questions are straightforward nonetheless.

Will is one of the nicest and most caring profs at this whole university. That being said, it doesn't seem like he plans his lectures. He's so knowledgable but the lectures are wild and all over the place. Assignments are actually pretty fun but beware of the grader who takes off points for literally anything. Exams are online and open for a week,

Ledbetter taught about 1/3 of my CNIT18200 class and the other professor taught the other 2/3. Ledbetter is a nice person and is willing to help when you ask for it (especially on project deliverables). However, his lectures can be boring and he can be scatter-brained at times; he seems to know his stuff, he just has to work on conveying it better.
Great Instructor!!

William Ledbetter is a graduate student, but he is a better instructor than most professors I have. He genuinely cares if the student learns the material and will try to find different ways to explain the material so that the student understands in the best way possible. Genuine guy; knowledgable from his experience in the field.

The professor is very knowledgeable about what he teaches and can answer students' questions to a satisfactory degree. His lecture notes were useful, and he assigned a reasonable homework load. His exams were reasonable. However, he disappeared right before midterms and the final to attend conferences, and had to hold multiple lectures online.

Professor Wlodarczyk is a fantastic PDE professor. His lectures are clear, well-structured, and surprisingly easy to follow given the subject of the class. His exams are very fair and reflect what he teaches in class and what he puts on the practice exams; no surprises. Plus, he gives exams during regular class time instead of in the evening.

There is a lot of material, so his lectures were usually just him listing theorems and proofs which came directly from the book. Memorizing theorems was important for the exams, and the homework was usually long and tedious. However, a lot of the material was similar to MA351, and he did an okay job with what he had to teach.

The lectures were intense but mostly clear. This is a grad level linear algebra course (mostly for engineering MS/PhD students who care about application), but most of the material was theoretical. Homeworks were roughly once per week and weren't too difficult. Exams were very similar to practice exams. Overall, a solid Professor.
The exams were very similar to the review problems that he gives, but they were also very long.
The review for the test contains wrong answers
The review session is highly related to the exams. The exam has T/F, multiple choices and theoretical proofs.

This guy knew his stuff very well, never had to prepare notes for his lectures.  Although homework assignment was somewhat heavy loaded, but since H.W grade was worth 1/3 of the total grade, the hw effort input was well worth it. Students can really enjoy going to his lectures and learn.

The lectures were usually ho-hum, but he is not a bad teacher. The homework was lengthy and challenging, but the tests were not very difficult -- the questions were very similar to his practice questions.

Writes incredibly fair exams that are similar to his practice exams, and he holds a review session the day before the exam, so make sure to go to that. More helpful in office hours than in class, I must admit. Mainly, because of his accent and because his handwriting is sloppy, but he does care that you learn the material.
A nice, approachable guy but his english can hardly be understood.

Lectures are fairly boring, but also incredibly fast paced. He tends to teach things in a different manner than the book. So if you don't understand his way, you might understand the book's. Very helpful outside of class. I STRONGLY recommend going to the review sessions before tests.

It takes a week or so to get used to his accent, but he tries very hard to teach his students.  He's a genuinely nice person, and will go out of his way to help you if you go to him one on one.  His exams are fairly easy.  One extra comment: I believe he only owns one or two pairs of pants...

Gets caught up in little details but doesn't present the bigger picture. Even though it's an applications class, he makes it all mathematical exercises with no practical purpose. I strongly suggest watching the book author's (Strang) online lectures before going to class, it will make a lot more sense. Also, I think he only owns one pair of pants.
He tries I'll give you that... but the lectures are dull and uninteresting.  At least he makes time for you outside of class.  The substitutes we had were FAR better than he was.
a great professor.  Tries very hard.   Very easy exams.
u can tell he tries... one of the easier to understand foreign teachers here, really nice about missing tests and stuff, tries to be understanding.
No Comments
Good instructor, explains things clearly. Exams are very tough, but he is a fair grader and is very relaxed about giving hw extensions. Course grades are curved as well.

Prof. Chakraborty is an excellent instructor who genuinely cares about student learning. He frequently connects concepts to real-world engineering problems. He is also very approachable and always willing to help. Exams are tough.
Nice guy, but the course is a huge drag and lectures are way too long and boring. straightforward A/A- though, just a ton of busy work.
Suman is a great lecturer and very knowledgeable, but it often feels li

Professor was borderline sadistic at times. Would make extremely difficult exams and smile as he walked through the aisle seeing students struggle. Assignments would be graded weeks after being turned in. Purdue prop specialization is so disorganized.
Extremely poor communication with students in the class for assignment requirements. Not aware of university policies.

He's a terrific lecturer and is very nice and approachable outside of class. However, the way he ran this course was awful. Homework and exam grades were often not given for months after the deadline, and the midterms for this class were genuinely impossible. He also assigned a literature review due 2 days before the final. I hope he improves.

Had Suman for an online course. He was terrible. Consistently late with posting assignments, content and grades.  Never provided feedback to the class for our final projects and assignments. It felt like he was rushed at the end of the course to make assignments up then he dumped a ton of work on us without much notice. Worst online course ever.
Solid professor. Lectures are a bit slow but also the course is quite light on content. Great at answering questions

Prof. Tulodziecki is extremely approachable and gives clear feedback on your work (like for the semester-long project) which is very helpful. She also explains fairly unintuitive logic concepts so clearly that it feels obvious.
Very personable when presenting. Direct, but not rude. Overall, strong professor that made learning first-order logic even more enjoyable.
I found the course material pretty tough, but the professor was really nice. She explains things well and genuinely listens to students. I liked her.

She really cares about all of her students and if you put in the effort the class will go fine for you. The lectures and the book pretty much tell you the exact same thing, but I found that her lectures are more concise and clear than the book. Most of the work done is homework problems through the book and these are also helpful. Great course.

Tulodziecki is a fantastic logic professor. She is extremely clear and very helpful. She is rather funny, and makes what could be a very dry topic interesting and enjoyable to learn. Her class is not easy, but that is not because of the exams. The homework tends to be much more difficult, but you can get help on that from her or a TA. She's Great!
The lectures were okay and everything he said made a lot of sense
Homeworks were on the longer side, but exams were fair and bonus was offered for attendance. Professor Hashemi lectured well.

Lectures were great, professor took time to answer all the questions by students. Homeworks were on a reasonable difficulty level (each homework took 1-2 days, total of 6 homeworks). Midterm was hard, but the grading of the course was very generous.

Insanely Heavy Workload! Avoid This Course If You Have Other Commitments! Don't say I didn't warn you!!!

ECE 69500OPT is one of the most demanding courses I've ever taken. The final project is even worse. If you're balancing job applications, internships, or your own research, I strongly advise against taking it. This course is a huge time sink!

This professor gives time to help time to help students and gives good advice in the lectures. With lectures being optional and them being helpful in understanding material, but the exams are still difficult. Many of my issues with them could've been solved by going to the physical lectures more and taking time to study more than I thought I needed
He was extremely rude when I asked for help and uncaring and inconsiderate of circumstances surrounding my performance.

Pretty chill teacher. Lecture are long because of the in class examples, but they definitely help. Tests have very few trick questions, but otherwise straightforward and the formula sheets cover a lot. Cares about you doing well and loves to help. Some topics are just more lackluster but he does his best to make the class enjoyable.
This guy is THE GOAT. Scott is amazing in every way possible. He actually saved my dog from a burning building while simultaneously lecturing about MGMT 201. What a guy.

He can be monotoned, but he is extremely helpful and very understanding. I had a situation during my exam, and he was extremely helpful, making me clam down and still have time to finish my exam. He wants to be an actual professor and knows students have situations so he helps rather than punishes them for being human beings.

Overall Scott is a great professor. He cares about his students a lot and wants them to succeed. Attendance is not mandatory but he gives extra credit if you come. The class if a little dry but its ok. I didn't do so well on the first exam but was able to bounce back by completing his practice exams and ask questions in class. Take him for MGMT 201

Sorensen is a very caring professor who wants his students to succeed.  He is very accessible if you ever have a question and he provides extra credit for attendance.  Accounting is a subject that is hard to make interesting, but I think he does a good job presenting the material in a very straightforward manner.

His lectures are really dry. Spends most of the lectures hunched over the document camera writing problems. Other than that the class itself isn't too hard. The exams and homework are pretty straightforward.

Overall a good professor. Teaches content well and in an easy to follow manor, but lectures aren't very engaging (its accounting). He is very kind and tries to be understanding. Overall a solid pick for someone genuinely trying to teach and help students.
He is a really understanding and nice professor. It was clear he wanted his students to succeed and do well in his class. He is understanding on extensions even after the deadline.

Thank God the class is over ! What a joke. Like others have said, Sorensen was sooo monotone and boring - I could hardly stay awake in class as he just went on and on and on. I understand this is it for him so that's good for all those taking it starting next school year - not soon enough as far as I am concerned. Purdue accounting is a joke.

I appreciated that he recorded lectures because I could not attend class without falling asleep.  The subject itself was fine because he gave us equation sheets for exams, but he was your typical boring accountant who was very monotone.
Nice guy, very monotone and boring class content. Provides a very helpful formula sheet for each exam. Class content is extremely boring and not practical outside of just getting a grade.
He was a very knowledgeable and helpful professor, but the lectures were just very boring.

I really enjoyed this class and Scott as a professor. I think accounting is fun so I didn't have a problem with the lectures being boring. He answered my emails quickly and answered any questions I had after class. He definitely cares about our success. The exams were not too hard and he provides a good formula sheet for each one. I recommend him!

Professor Sorensen clearly cares about what he teaches and his students' success. I'm repeating others here, but yes, while his lectures do feel boring, that's just due to the content itself. There's not a lot you can do to make accounting exciting. Homework is not bad, and formula sheets on the exams were very helpful.

Professor Sorensen was very nice and clearly explained things, however, he was very monotone which made lectures difficult for me personally. He provided formula sheets for exams which were super helpful, and he posts the lectures online so attendance wasn't required which was nice.

Professor Sorensen is very nice and understanding! There is only a homework assignment per week. All other points for the class are exams. He gives extra credit during random lectures if you are there. If you know the concepts and how to do the problems done in class you will do well on the exams! He responds fast to emails too.

Great professor and super nice guy, but the class itself is pretty boring. 1 point of extra credit randomly for attendance. Class is manageable and professor is very understanding and accommodates to needs of students. Definitely recommend doing homework to pad your grade - HW is significantly easier than mgmt200.
He is willing to help if you reach out to him. He answers any questions that are given out, and provides a formula sheet with the exams.
Professor is intelligent and nice, but lecture can become boring and distracting at times

I was nervous to take the class going into it because of some of the reviews, but the professor does care about your success if you reach out when needed. He mentioned they restructured the class based on previous years reviews. Only bad part was lectures were hard to hear unless you sat up front.

I really liked Professor Sorenson. I accidentally did the wrong homework once and he gave me a 100% for the assignment I missed. He is very understanding, you just have to reach out to him. He gives lots of practice questions and the exams were online with a 24 hr window to take them. Very caring professor and definitely one of my favorites!
Professor Sorensen is pretty nice. He gave us a lots of practice example in the class.
Good communicator, class is 2/3 exams though and they're certainly no joke
Just a chill funny guy

Rubin is an amazing teacher! Lecture heavy but he makes the lectures funny and teaches everything very well. He knows what he's talking about and is very approachable if you have questions. Definitely take this class if you're starting or thinking about a major in Actuarial Sciences. Have a day!

Genuinely the greatest professor at Purdue, he truly cares about his students and makes you look forward to his lectures. He's very funny and makes boring info really interesting. Very little work to do and easy exams if you pay attention in lectures. Attendance is pretty much mandatory.

Dan is a very amusing professor who is great at making traditionally "boring" concepts interesting. There are only a few HW assignments, and 7 excel labs. The labs greatly improved my understanding of how to use excel. 
Make sure to attend lecture because Dan is a stickler for attendance, he will make a public scene if you're late or absent.

Dan is a pretty good lecturer that brings a lot of experience. My only complains would be that he would get quite annoyed at little things (like someone being a few minutes late) and make a big deal out of them. He can also get frustrated if you do not understand something quickly and sometimes has trouble understanding questions; but overall good.

Professor Rubin is a great prof and always willing to help out with understanding concepts during office hours. Nice intro course to actuarial science and what you could potentially do as an actuary in the future if you decide to go that route. I'd recommend his class to any math major.

Dan is extremely helpful and brings a lot of industry experience. Says a lot that isn't written down, so go to every class if possible. There are 2/3 one-on-ones you have to have with him during the semester, but they were helpful and a chance to ask questions beyond just the class.

Dan is a great teacher who is engaged in the material he teaches. Sometimes, he doesn't go into depth about certain notes, but you shouldn't be tested on them. He made class fun and something to look forward to.

Dan is the sweetest man ever. Great sense of humor and super caring of all his students. The class has basically no homework and the labs are very manageable. The class lectures are honestly pretty boring but his sense of humor makes it more entertaining. Attendance is important and lots of stuff from lectures are on exams.

Extremely good and funny professor. He had a long Actuary career before becoming a professor at Purdue, and uses his real experience to help convey his points. Since MA170 is an intro course, he provided a lot of insight as to whether being an actuary is good for the student.

I took his intro to ac. science class and he is a VERY funny guy. He tries to make the lectures interesting but some of the material is confusing. He does require you to attend class for part of your grade. One criticism I have of his class is that he does not do enough practice problems/homework to help students prepare for the exams.

He is a great professor! I didn't stay in actuarial science, but I still thought this class was helpful in understanding the basics of actuarial science! He has engaging lectures and is a very fair professor! Exams are pretty easy!

Homework, especially Chapters 3 and 13 was awful. He doesn't teach the material in an applicable use or example, so you're just stuck wondering how to do the homework for a while until you get it from someone else, go to Office Hours, or magically figure it out. The lectures at first were good until he transitioned to slides after chapter 6 sucked.
He's the best. He makes class engaging and cares about what he does.

I loved Dan! Made the class entertaining and was invested in everyone's individual learning. He was very patient and informative during office hours and teaches the material effectively. Seems passionate about what he does and is happy to help students out.

I was able to understand the material pretty well for most of the course until the end (his slideshow presentations on difficult topics were not that great). He is kind of tough on you and can be rude sometimes, but he grades fairly and tries to make the class fair. Watch out for the syllabus pop quiz, and study a lot for the quizzes and exams.
Dan's lectures were very informative and entertaining!
Great professor, explains the content well. Also lets you out early a lot

Professor Rubin is a very good teacher. Going to the lectures is a requirement because he does take attendance. He cares about his students because he makes them go to a 1 on 1 meeting once a month to talk to them about the class and actuarial science. The guest speakers he brings in sometimes is very helpful as well so you can see actuaries irl.
Great professor, LUVs to give practical examples of the material we learnt in class.

He is a former actuary so he has a lot of important insight for people considering the profession. He brings in a lot of speakers that really add to the class. His tests can be tough, but they should not be a problem if you pay attention and study. Overall, he is a really nice guy who makes lectures interesting.

Dan is great, class is fairly easy yet informative on actuarial science. Homework and Labs are NOT the same weight as the tests (read the syllabus people) but are easy. Dan is really nice and will answer any questions you have including questions on the field as a whole and will even do 1 on 1s every month which is part of you attendance grade.

Easy A if you really put in the effort into your labs and homework. Homeworks and labs have the same weight as a test which is pretty sweet. Attendance is VERY IMPORTANT! After really putting in effort into my labs and homework and scoring decently on the midterm, I could've gotten a 0 on the final and gotten an A-.

Dan is a great guy. He is very helpful with any questions you have, related to class content or just in general about actuarial science. Great at explaining concepts but you do have to study to memorize specifics. Homework and labs were easy and had a fair weight on our grades which was nice. Overall, I loved him!

I enjoyed Professor Rubin's lectures this semester and felt he did a good job teaching material and keeping our attention with his various stories. The homework and labs are not hard and tests aren't either. This will probably be the easiest actuarial science class you take. If you can't pass with at minimum a C, it's time to look for a new major.

Maybe I'm just slow but I didn't get a lot of things explained in lecture but he is a funny guy, mainly how he says "hueh" at the end of some of his sentences. This makes up for the fact I go into class and don't understand a single thing and I study it myself. I'm letting it slide because the class isn't hard to self study.

Good professor, lectures, explanation, and handwriting. For MA527 Summer session it is a lecture and homework assignment for every weekday (due sunday). Get ready to work a lot outside of class time to keep up if you aren't already familiar in the topics taught. Class is taught fully from one textbook and it is easy enough to follow along.

I would highly recommend taking MA303 with Professor Park. The content gets very conceptually challenging once you get late into the semester, but if you make it a point to never miss a class and stay on top of homework, you can do well. He also allows the use of crib sheets on the final exam, which to my knowledge, no other PDE professor does.

Answers question in Piazza 24/7. Works out a lot of examples, makes sure periodically that the students are understanding concepts, does not hesitate to go back a few steps, problems worked out during lectures which are not in the textbook appear on the tests a lot, so lecture notes are equally as important as homework and practice test questions.

Open book & notes, multiple choice midterm and gives partial credit if the process is correct and incorrect answers or selected incorrect answer by mistake. Test questions very similar to practice exams and examples he works out in lectures and some homework problems. KEY - MORE QUESTIONS FROM TOPICS COVERED RIGHT BEFORE MIDTERM AND FINALS.

Gives a bonus project (4%) and pre-requisite test (1%), which is a half a letter extra credit. I didn't do them due to lack of time over Summer and still managed to get A-. He curves heavily so that at the end the class average becomes A-.

Professor Park is an amazing professor, he lectures MA303 very well and teaches concepts clearly. Additionally, he is generous about the use of cheat sheets which alleviates the need to memorize formulas and allows you to focus on important concepts rather than memorization. His passion for the subject is also evident which helps you stay focused.

Professor Moongyu is great. He makes difficult concepts easy to grasp, even the challenging ones. He pays attention to us and is always available on Piazza to answer questions and discuss our ideas. This proves he truly cares about our success. He's also respectful and friendly when interacting with students, which greatly makes me respect him.

A kind professor with organized lectures and timely exam feedback. He posted extra videos containing helpful examples. His accent is a nonissue. Midterm and final averages were 68 and 70, respectively.

(Spring 2022)

Dr. Park was an excellent lecturer for MA266. He has a slight accent but he is still very understandable and if he says anything in an unclearly he usually goes back over it. He does his best to reiterate on things.

His exams were fair but still difficult, and my resulting grades were more my lack of work ethic than his teachings.

Class was overall easy if you understood MA266, aka easy to relearn the initial content and then Park did a good job going over the new concepts. Sometimes misunderstood questions and made the two midterms a little bit tricky, but overall a good professor. Good class if you put in the work.

Park assigns a lot of homework to ensure a good understanding of the material, although a portion is graded by completion.  Exams have no partial credit so a minor mistake can harm your grade.  Finally, Park does respond quickly to questions, but he tends to put little effort into his answers and doesn't give complete responses.

He is unable to clearly communicate material. Didnt provide too many examples, worst math textbook and assigned 20+ homework problems each week, it was not an enjoyable class. The tests were multiple choice and lengthy and no partial credit was awarded. Grading was also inconsistent. Online presence was also lacking

Class was mostly a recap of MA266 material, so it was easy to relearn, regardless of who was teaching. Frequently misunderstood questions and was not clear in some explanations of concepts, but did go rather in depth into others, which enhanced understanding of previous material. Difficult exams with high curves
